question_text: Calculate the mole fraction of ethylene glycol (C 2 H 6 O 2 ) in a solution containing 20% of C 2 H 6 O 2 by mass.
question_html: Calculate the mole fraction of ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) in a solution containing 20% of C2H6O2 by mass.
solution_text: Let total mass of solution = 100 g Mass of ethylene glycol W 2 = 20 g Mass of solvent (water) W 1 = 80 g Molar mass of [C 2 H 6 O 2 ] M 1 = {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{(12\;\times\;2)\;+(1\;\times6)\;+(2\;\times16)}{/tex} = 24 + 6 + 32 = 62 g mol -1 Molar mass of water (H 2 O) M 2 = 18g mol -1 Molar of water {tex}{n_1} = \frac{{{W_1}}}{{{M_1}}} = \frac{{80}}{{18}} = 4.44\,mole{/tex} Moles of C 2 H 6 O 2 {tex}{n_2} = \frac{{{W_2}}}{{{M_2}}} = \frac{{20}}{{62}}{/tex} = 0.322 mol Mole fraction of {tex}{C_2}{H_6}{O_2} = \frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1} + {n_2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.322}}{{4.44 + 0.322}}{/tex} = 0.068
solution_html:
Let total mass of solution = 100 g
Mass of ethylene glycol W2 = 20 g
Mass of solvent (water) W1 = 80 g
Molar mass of [C2H6O2]
M1 = {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{(12\;\times\;2)\;+(1\;\times6)\;+(2\;\times16)}{/tex}
= 24 + 6 + 32
= 62 g mol-1
Molar mass of water (H2O)  
M2= 18g mol-1
Molar of water {tex}{n_1} = \frac{{{W_1}}}{{{M_1}}} = \frac{{80}}{{18}} = 4.44\,mole{/tex}
Moles of C2H6O2
{tex}{n_2} = \frac{{{W_2}}}{{{M_2}}} = \frac{{20}}{{62}}{/tex}
= 0.322 mol
Mole fraction of {tex}{C_2}{H_6}{O_2} = \frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1} + {n_2}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.322}}{{4.44 + 0.322}}{/tex} = 0.068

question_text: Calculate the molarity of a solution containing 5 g of NaOH in 450 mL solution.
question_html: Calculate the molarity of a solution containing 5 g of NaOH in 450 mL solution.
solution_text: Moles of NaOH = {tex}\frac{5 \mathrm{g}}{40 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.125 mol Volume of the solution in litres = {tex}\frac {450 \ mL} {1000 \ mL \ {L}^{-1}} {/tex} Using equation of the molarity Molarity = {tex}\frac{0.125 \mathrm{mol} \times 1000 \mathrm{mL} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{450 \mathrm{mL}}{/tex} = m = 0.278 mol L -1 = 0.278 mol dm -3
solution_html:
Moles of NaOH = {tex}\frac{5 \mathrm{g}}{40 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.125 mol
Volume of the solution in litres = {tex}\frac {450 \ mL} {1000 \ mL \ {L}^{-1}} {/tex}
Using equation of the molarity
Molarity = {tex}\frac{0.125 \mathrm{mol} \times 1000 \mathrm{mL} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{450 \mathrm{mL}}{/tex} = m
= 0.278 mol L-1
= 0.278 mol dm-3

question_text: Calculate molality of 2.5 g of ethanoic acid (CH 3 COOH) in 75 g of benzene.
question_html: Calculate molality of 2.5 g of ethanoic acid (CH3COOH) in 75 g of benzene.
solution_text: Given, mass of ethanoic acid = 2.5g Molar mass of C 2 H 4 O 2 : 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 2+1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4+16 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 60 g mol -1 Moles of C 2 H 4 O 2 = {tex}\frac{2.5 \mathrm{g}}{60 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.0417 mol Mass of benzene in kg = 75 g / 1000 g kg -1 = 75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 kg Molality of C 2 H 4 O 2 = {tex}\frac{\text { Moles of } \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{2}}{\text { kg of benzene }}=\frac{0.0417 \mathrm{mol} \times 1000 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}}{75 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 0.556 mol kg -1
solution_html:
Given, mass of ethanoic acid = 2.5g

Molar mass of C2H4O2: 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 2+1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4+16 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 60 g mol-1
Moles of C2 H4 O2 = {tex}\frac{2.5 \mathrm{g}}{60 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.0417 mol
Mass of benzene in kg = 75 g / 1000 g kg-1 = 75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 kg
Molality of C2 H4 O2 = {tex}\frac{\text { Moles of } \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{2}}{\text { kg of benzene }}=\frac{0.0417 \mathrm{mol} \times 1000 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}}{75 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
= 0.556 mol kg-1

question_text: If N 2 gas is bubbled through water at 293 K, how many millimoles of N 2 gas would dissolve in 1 L of water? Assume that N 2 exerts a partial pressure of 0.987 bar. Given that Henry's law constant for N 2 at 293 K is 76.48 K bar.
question_html: If N2 gas is bubbled through water at 293 K, how many millimoles of N2 gas would dissolve in 1 L of water? Assume that N2 exerts a partial pressure of 0.987 bar. Given that Henry's law constant for N2 at 293 K is 76.48 K bar.
solution_text: According to Henry's law, p N2 = K H {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} N 2 {tex}\chi{/tex} N 2 = {tex}\frac{p_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.987 \mathrm \ {bar}}{76480 \mathrm \ {bar}}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 If n moles of N 2 are present in 1L or 1000 g of water (or {tex}\frac{10008}{188 \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 55.5 moles) {tex}\chi{/tex} 2 = {tex}\frac{n}{n+55.5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} (as n < < < 55.5) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.5 moles = 71.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 moles = 0.716 moles
solution_html:
According to Henry's law,
pN2 = KH {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex}N2
{tex}\chi{/tex}N2 = {tex}\frac{p_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.987 \mathrm \ {bar}}{76480 \mathrm \ {bar}}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
If n moles of N2 are present in 1L or 1000 g of
water (or {tex}\frac{10008}{188 \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 55.5 moles)
{tex}\chi{/tex}2 = {tex}\frac{n}{n+55.5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} (as n < < < 55.5)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.5 moles
= 71.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 moles = 0.716 moles

question_text: Vapour pressure of chloroform (CHCl 3 ) and dichloromethane (CH 2 Cl 2 ) at 298 K are 200 mm Hg and 415 mm Hg respectively. Calculate the vapour pressure of the solution prepared by mixing 25.5 g of CHCl 3 and 40 g of CH 2 Cl 2 at 298 K and mole fractions of each component in vapour phase.
question_html:
Vapour pressure of chloroform (CHCl3) and dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) at 298 K are 200 mm Hg and 415 mm Hg respectively.

  1. Calculate the vapour pressure of the solution prepared by mixing 25.5 g of CHCl3 and 40 g of CH2Cl2 at 298 K and
  2. mole fractions of each component in vapour phase.
solution_text: Given that vapour pressure of CHCl 3 = 200mmHg CH 2 Cl 2 = 415mmHg Molar mass of CH 2 Cl 2 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 35.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 85 g mol -1 Molar mass of CHCl 3 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 35.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 119.5 g mol -1 Moles of CH 2 Cl 2 = {tex}\frac{40 \mathrm{g}}{85 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.47 mol Moles of CHCl 3 = {tex}\frac{25.5 \mathrm{g}}{119.5 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.213 mol Total number of moles = 0.47 + 0.213 = 0.683 mol {tex}x_{\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}=\frac{0.47 \mathrm{mol}}{0.683 \mathrm{mol}}{/tex} = 0.688 {tex}x_{\mathrm{CllC}_{3}}{/tex} = 1.00 - 0.688 = 0.312 Using equation of partial vapour pressure we get, p total = {tex}p_{1}^{0}+\left(p_{2}^{0}-p_{1}^{0}\right) x_{2}{/tex} = 200 + (415 - 200) {tex}\times{/tex} 0.688 = 200 + 147.9 = 347.9 mm Hg Using the relation, y 1 = p 1 / p total , we can calculate the mole fraction of the components in gas phase (y). {tex}p_{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{/tex} = 0.688 {tex}\times{/tex} 415 mm Hg = 285.5 mm Hg {tex}p_{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}{/tex} = 0.312 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 mm Hg = 62.4 mm Hg {tex}y_{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{/tex} = 285.5 mm Hg / 347.9 mm Hg = 0.82 {tex}y_{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}{/tex} = 62.4 mm Hg / 347.9 mm Hg = 0.18 Note: Since, CH 2 Cl 2 is a more volatile component than CHCl 3 , [ {tex}p_{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}^{0}{/tex} = 415 mm Hg and {tex}p_{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}^{O}{/tex} = 200 mmHg] and the vapour phase is also richerin CH 2 Cl 2 [ {tex}\mathcal{Y}_{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{/tex} = 0.82 and {tex}\mathcal{Y}_{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}{/tex} = 0.181], it may thus be concluded that at equilibrium, vapour phase will be always rich in the component which is more volatile.
solution_html:
Given that vapour pressure of CHCl3 = 200mmHg
CH2Cl2 = 415mmHg

  1. Molar mass of CH2Cl2 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 35.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 85 g mol-1
    Molar mass of CHCl3 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 35.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 119.5 g mol-1
    Moles of CH2 Cl2 = {tex}\frac{40 \mathrm{g}}{85 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex}= 0.47 mol
    Moles of CHCl3 = {tex}\frac{25.5 \mathrm{g}}{119.5 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.213 mol
    Total number of moles = 0.47 + 0.213 = 0.683 mol
    {tex}x_{\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}=\frac{0.47 \mathrm{mol}}{0.683 \mathrm{mol}}{/tex} = 0.688
    {tex}x_{\mathrm{CllC}_{3}}{/tex} = 1.00 - 0.688 = 0.312
    Using equation of partial vapour pressure we get,
    ptotal = {tex}p_{1}^{0}+\left(p_{2}^{0}-p_{1}^{0}\right) x_{2}{/tex} = 200 + (415 - 200) {tex}\times{/tex} 0.688 
    = 200 + 147.9 = 347.9 mm Hg
  2. Using the relation, y1 = p1 / ptotal, we can calculate the mole fraction of the components in gas phase (y).
    {tex}p_{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{/tex} = 0.688 {tex}\times{/tex} 415 mm Hg = 285.5 mm Hg
    {tex}p_{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}{/tex} = 0.312 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 mm Hg = 62.4 mm Hg
    {tex}y_{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{/tex}= 285.5 mm Hg / 347.9 mm Hg = 0.82
    {tex}y_{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}{/tex} = 62.4 mm Hg / 347.9 mm Hg = 0.18

Note: Since, CH2Cl2 is a more volatile component than CHCl3, [{tex}p_{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}^{0}{/tex} = 415 mm Hg and {tex}p_{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}^{O}{/tex} = 200 mmHg] and the vapour phase is also richerin CH2Cl2 [{tex}\mathcal{Y}_{\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{/tex} = 0.82 and {tex}\mathcal{Y}_{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}{/tex} = 0.181], it may thus be concluded that at equilibrium, vapour phase will be always rich in the component which is more volatile.

question_text: The vapour pressure of pure benzene at a certain temperature is 0.850 bar. A non-volatile, non-electrolyte solid weighing 0.5 g when added to 39.0 g of benzene (molar mass 78 g mol -1 ). Vapour pressure of the solution, then, is 0.845 bar. What is the molar mass of the solid substance?
question_html: The vapour pressure of pure benzene at a certain temperature is 0.850 bar. A non-volatile, non-electrolyte solid weighing 0.5 g when added to 39.0 g of benzene (molar mass 78 g mol-1). Vapour pressure of the solution, then, is 0.845 bar. What is the molar mass of the solid substance?
solution_text: The various quantities known to us are as follows: p 1 0 = 0.850 bar; p = 0.845 bar; M 1 = 78g mol -1 ; w 2 = 0.5 g; w 1 = 39 g Substituting these values in equation of relative lowering of vapour pressure, we get {tex}\frac{0.850 \text { bar }-0.845 \text { bar }}{0.850 \text { bar }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.5 \mathrm{g} \times 78 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{M_{2} \times 39 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} Therefore, M 2 = 170 g mol -1
solution_html:
The various quantities known to us are as follows:
p10 = 0.850 bar; p = 0.845 bar; M1 = 78g mol-1; w2 = 0.5 g; w1 = 39 g
Substituting these values in equation of relative lowering of vapour pressure, we get
{tex}\frac{0.850 \text { bar }-0.845 \text { bar }}{0.850 \text { bar }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.5 \mathrm{g} \times 78 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{M_{2} \times 39 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
Therefore, M2 = 170 g mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_text: 18 g of glucose, C 6 H 12 O 6 , is dissolved in 1 kg of water in a saucepan. At what temperature will water boil at 1.013 bar? K b for water is 0.52 K kg mol -1 .
question_html: 18 g of glucose, C6H12O6, is dissolved in 1 kg of water in a saucepan. At what temperature will water boil at 1.013 bar? Kb for water is 0.52 K kg mol-1.
solution_text: Moles of glucose = {tex}\frac {18\ g}{180 \ gmol^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.1 mol Number of kilograms of solvent = 1 kg Thus molality of glucose solution = 0.1 mol kg -1 For water, change in boiling point The elevation (T b ) in boiling point is {tex}\Delta T_{\mathrm{b}}=K_{\mathrm{b}} \times m{/tex} = 0.52 K kg mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 mol kg -1 = 0.052 k Since water boils at 373.15 K at 1.013 bar pressure, therefore, the boiling point of the solution will be 373.15 + 0.052 = 373.202 K.
solution_html:
Moles of glucose = {tex}\frac {18\ g}{180 \ gmol^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.1 mol 
Number of kilograms of solvent = 1 kg
Thus molality of glucose solution = 0.1 mol kg-1 For water, change in boiling point
The elevation (Tb) in boiling point is
{tex}\Delta T_{\mathrm{b}}=K_{\mathrm{b}} \times m{/tex} = 0.52 K kg mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 mol kg-1 = 0.052 k
Since water boils at 373.15 K at 1.013 bar pressure, therefore, the boiling point of the solution will be 373.15 + 0.052 = 373.202 K.

question_text: The boiling point of benzene is 353.23 K. When 1.80 g of a non-volatile solute was dissolved in 90 g of benzene, the boiling point is raised to 354.11 K. Calculate the molar mass of the solute. K b for benzene is 2.53 K kg mol –1 .
question_html: The boiling point of benzene is 353.23 K. When 1.80 g of a non-volatile solute was dissolved in 90 g of benzene, the boiling point is raised to 354.11 K. Calculate the molar mass of the solute. Kb for benzene is 2.53 K kg mol–1.
solution_text: The elevation {tex}\left(\Delta T_{\mathrm{b}}\right){/tex} in the boiling point = 354.11 K - 353.23 K = 0.88 K Substituting these values in the following equation we get M 2 = {tex}\frac{2.53 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1.8 \mathrm{g} \times 1000 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}}{0.88 \mathrm{K} \times 90 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 58 g mol -1 Therefore, the molar mass of the solute, M 2 = 58 g mol -1 .
solution_html:
The elevation {tex}\left(\Delta T_{\mathrm{b}}\right){/tex} in the boiling point = 354.11 K - 353.23 K = 0.88 K
Substituting these values in the following equation we get
M2 = {tex}\frac{2.53 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1.8 \mathrm{g} \times 1000 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}}{0.88 \mathrm{K} \times 90 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 58 g mol-1
Therefore, the molar mass of the solute, M2 = 58 g mol-1.

question_text: 45 g of ethylene glycol (C 2 H 6 O 2 ) is mixed with 600 g of water. Calculate the freezing point depression and the freezing point of the solution.
question_html:
45 g of ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) is mixed with 600 g of water. Calculate

  1. the freezing point depression and
  2. the freezing point of the solution.
solution_text: Given, W 1 = 600 g, K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 W 2 = 45 g, M 2 = 62.0 g mol -1 T {tex}_f^o{/tex} (water) = 273 k {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.86 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 45 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{62 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 600 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2.25 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - T f T f = 273 K - 2.25 K = 270.75 K
solution_html:
Given, W1 = 600 g, Kf = 1.86 K kg mol-1
W2 = 45 g, M2 = 62.0 g mol-1
T{tex}_f^o{/tex} (water) = 273 k

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1.86 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 45 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{62 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 600 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 2.25 K
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = T{tex}_f^o{/tex} - Tf
    Tf = 273 K - 2.25 K = 270.75 K

{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0.40 K, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex}
M2 = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{W_1}\, \times \,{\Delta T_f}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{5.12\, \times \,1\, \times \,1000}}{{50\, \times \,0.40}}{/tex}

question_text: 2 g of benzoic acid (C 6 H 5 COOH) dissolved in 25 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point equal to 1.62 K. Molal depression constant for benzene is 4.9 K kg mol –1 . What is the percentage association of acid if it forms a dimer in solution?
question_html: 2 g of benzoic acid (C6H5COOH) dissolved in 25 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point equal to 1.62 K. Molal depression constant for benzene is 4.9 K kg mol–1. What is the percentage association of acid if it forms a dimer in solution?
solution_text: The given quantities are: w 2 = 2g; (Molar depression constant of benzene) K f = 4.9 K kg mol -1 ; w 1 = 25 g Depression in freezing point equal to {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = 1.62 K Substituting these values in equation (2.36) we get: M 1 = {tex}\frac{4.9 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 2 \mathrm{g} \times 1000 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}}{25 \mathrm{g} \times 1.62 \mathrm{K}}{/tex} = 241.98 g mol -1 Thus, the experimental molar mass of benzoic acid in benzene is = 241.98 g mol -1 Now consider the following equilibrium for the acid: 2C 6 H 5 COOH} {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (C 6 H 5 {COOH) 2 If x represents the degree of association of the solute then we would have (1 - x) mol of benzoic acid left in unassociated form and correspondingly {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} as associated moles of benzoic acid at equilibrium. Therefore, the total number of moles of particles at equilibrium is: 1 - x + {tex}\frac{x}{2}=1-\frac{x}{2}{/tex} Thus, the total number of moles of particles at equilibrium equals van't Hoff factor i. But i = {tex}\frac{\text { Normal molar mass }}{\text { Abnormal molar mass }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{122 \mathrm{gmol}^{-1}}{241.98 \mathrm{gmol}^{-1}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x}{2}=1-\frac{122}{241.98}{/tex} = 1 - 0.504 = 0.496 or x = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.496 = 0.992 Therefore, degree of association of benzoic acid in benzene is 99.2 %

2C6H5COOH} {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (C6H5{COOH)2

or {tex}\frac{x}{2}=1-\frac{122}{241.98}{/tex} = 1 - 0.504 = 0.496
or x = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.496 = 0.992
Therefore, degree of association of benzoic acid in benzene is 99.2 %

subject: Chemistry
question_text: 0.6 mL of acetic acid (CH 3 COOH), having density 1.06 g mL -1 , is dissolved in 1 litre of water. The depression in freezing point observed for this strength of acid was 0.0205 o C. Calculate the van’t Hoff factor and the dissociation constant of acid.
question_html: 0.6 mL of acetic acid (CH3COOH), having density 1.06 g mL-1, is dissolved in 1 litre of water. The depression in freezing point observed for this strength of acid was 0.0205oC. Calculate the van’t Hoff factor and the dissociation constant of acid.
solution_text: Given that, 0.6mL of acetic acid (CH 3 COOH) density = 1.06gmL -1 Number of moles of acetic acid = {tex}\frac{0.6 \mathrm{mL} \times 1.06 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mL}^{-1}}{60 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.0106 mol = n Molality = {tex}\frac{0.0106 \mathrm{mol}}{1000 \mathrm{mL} \times 1 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mL}^{-1}} {/tex} = 0.0106 mol kg -1 Using equation of depression of freezing point {tex}\Delta T_{\mathrm{f}}{/tex} = 1.86 K kg mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0106 mol kg -1 = 0.0197K van't Hoff Factor (i) = {tex}\frac{\text { Observed freezing point }}{\text { Calculated freezing point }}=\frac{0.0205 \mathrm{K}}{0.0197 \mathrm{K}}{/tex} = 1.041 Acetic acid is a weak electrolyte and will dissociate into two ions: acetate and hydrogen ions per molecule of acetic acid. If x is the degree of dissociation of acetic acid, then we would have n(1 - x) moles of undissociated acetic acid, nx moles of CH 3 COO - and nx moles of H + ions, {tex}\underset{n(1-x)}{\underset{n\;mol}{CH_3COOH}}\;\rightleftharpoons\underset{nx\;mol}{\underset0{\;H^+}}+\underset{nx\;mol}{\underset0{CH_3COO^-}}{/tex} Thus total moles of particles are: n(1 - x + x + x) = n(1 + x) i = {tex}\frac{n(1+x)}{n}{/tex} = 1 + x = 1.041 Thus degree of dissociation of acetic acid = x = 1.041 - 1.000 = 0.041 Then [CH 3 COOH] = n(1 - x) = 0.0106(1 - 0.041) [CH 3 COO - ] = nx = 0.0106 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.041, [H + ] = nx = 0.0106 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.041} K a = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}=\frac{0.0106 \times 0.041 \times 0.0106 \times 0.041}{0.0106(1.00-0.041)} {/tex} =1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5
solution_html:
Given that, 0.6mL of acetic acid (CH3COOH) density = 1.06gmL-1
Number of moles of acetic acid = {tex}\frac{0.6 \mathrm{mL} \times 1.06 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mL}^{-1}}{60 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.0106 mol = n
Molality = {tex}\frac{0.0106 \mathrm{mol}}{1000 \mathrm{mL} \times 1 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mL}^{-1}} {/tex} = 0.0106 mol kg-1
Using equation of depression of freezing point  {tex}\Delta T_{\mathrm{f}}{/tex} = 1.86 K kg mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0106 mol kg-1 = 0.0197K
van't Hoff Factor (i) = {tex}\frac{\text { Observed freezing point }}{\text { Calculated freezing point }}=\frac{0.0205 \mathrm{K}}{0.0197 \mathrm{K}}{/tex} = 1.041
Acetic acid is a weak electrolyte and will dissociate into two ions:
acetate and hydrogen ions per molecule of acetic acid. If x is the degree of dissociation of acetic acid, then we would have n(1 - x) moles of undissociated acetic acid, nx moles of CH3COO- and nx moles of H+ ions,
{tex}\underset{n(1-x)}{\underset{n\;mol}{CH_3COOH}}\;\rightleftharpoons\underset{nx\;mol}{\underset0{\;H^+}}+\underset{nx\;mol}{\underset0{CH_3COO^-}}{/tex} 
Thus total moles of particles are: n(1 - x + x + x) = n(1 + x)
i = {tex}\frac{n(1+x)}{n}{/tex} = 1 + x = 1.041
Thus degree of dissociation of acetic acid = x = 1.041 - 1.000 = 0.041
Then [CH3COOH] = n(1 - x) = 0.0106(1 - 0.041)
[CH3COO-] = nx = 0.0106 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.041, [H+] = nx = 0.0106 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.041}
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}=\frac{0.0106 \times 0.041 \times 0.0106 \times 0.041}{0.0106(1.00-0.041)} {/tex}
=1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

question_text: Calculate the mass percentage of benzene (C 6 H 6 ) and carbon tetrachloride (CCl 4 ) if 22 g of benzene is dissolved in 122 g of carbon tetrachloride.
question_html: Calculate the mass percentage of benzene (C6H6) and carbon tetrachloride (CCl4) if 22 g of benzene is dissolved in 122 g of carbon tetrachloride.
solution_text: Mass of solution = Mass of benzene (C 6 H 6 ) + Mass of CCl 4 = 22 g + 122 g = 144 g Mass percentage of {tex}{C_6}{H_6} = \frac{{{\text{Mass of benzene}}}}{{{\text{Mass of solution}}}} \times 100{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{22g}}{{144g}} \times 100{/tex} = 15.28% Mass percentage of {tex}CC{l_4} = \frac{{122g}}{{144g}} \times 100{/tex} = 84.72% or 100-15.28 = 84.72%
solution_html:
Mass of solution = Mass of benzene (C6H6) + Mass of CCl4
= 22 g + 122 g = 144 g
Mass percentage of {tex}{C_6}{H_6} = \frac{{{\text{Mass of benzene}}}}{{{\text{Mass of solution}}}} \times 100{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{22g}}{{144g}} \times 100{/tex} = 15.28%

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the mole fraction of benzene in solution containing 30% by mass in carbon tetrachloride.
question_html: Calculate the mole fraction of benzene in solution containing 30% by mass in carbon tetrachloride.
solution_text: Let the total mass of the solution be 100 g and the mass of benzene be 30 g. ∴Mass of carbon tetrachloride = (100 - 30) g = 70 g Molar mass of benzene (C 6 H 6 ) {tex} = (6 \times 12 + 6)g\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} = 78 g mol -1 ∴ Number of moles of {tex}{C_6}{H_6} = \frac{{30}}{{78}}mol{/tex} = 0.3846 mol Molar mass of carbon tetrachloride {tex}(CC{l_4}) = 1 \times 12 + 4 \times 35.5{/tex} = 154 g mol -1 ∴Number of moles of {tex}CC{l_4} = \frac{{70}}{{154}}mol{/tex} = 0.4545 mol Thus, the mole fraction of C 6 H 6 is given as: {tex}\frac{{Number\;of\;moles\;of\;{C_6}{H_6}}}{{Number\;of\;moles\;of\;{C_6}{H_6} + Number\;of\;moles\;of\;CC{l_4}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.3846}}{{0.3846 + 0.4545}}{/tex} = 0.458
solution_html:
Let the total mass of the solution be 100 g and the mass of benzene be 30 g.
∴Mass of carbon tetrachloride = (100 - 30) g
= 70 g
Molar mass of benzene (C6H6){tex} = (6 \times 12 + 6)g\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
= 78 g mol-1
∴ Number of moles of {tex}{C_6}{H_6} = \frac{{30}}{{78}}mol{/tex}
= 0.3846 mol
Molar mass of carbon tetrachloride {tex}(CC{l_4}) = 1 \times 12 + 4 \times 35.5{/tex}
= 154 g mol-1
∴Number of moles of {tex}CC{l_4} = \frac{{70}}{{154}}mol{/tex}
= 0.4545 mol
Thus, the mole fraction of C6H6is given as:
{tex}\frac{{Number\;of\;moles\;of\;{C_6}{H_6}}}{{Number\;of\;moles\;of\;{C_6}{H_6} + Number\;of\;moles\;of\;CC{l_4}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.3846}}{{0.3846 + 0.4545}}{/tex}
= 0.458

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the molarity of each of the following solutions: 30 g of Co(NO 3 ) 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 6H 2 O in 4.3 L of solution 30 mL of 0.5 M H 2 SO 4 diluted to 500 mL.
question_html:
Calculate the molarity of each of the following solutions:

  1. 30 g of Co(NO3)2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 6H2O in 4.3 L of solution
  2. 30 mL of 0.5 M H2SO4 diluted to 500 mL.
solution_text: Molarity is given by: Molarity {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume solution in litre}}}}{/tex} Molar mass of Co(NO 3 ) 2 6H 2 O = 59 + 2 (14 + 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 16) + (6 {tex} \times {/tex} 18)= 291 g mol -1 Therefore, Moles of {tex}Co{(N{O_3})_2} \cdot 6{H_2}O{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{30}}{{291}}{/tex} mol = 0.103 mol Therefore, molarity {tex} = \frac{{0.103mol}}{{4.3L}}{/tex} = 0.023 M Number of moles present in 1000 mL of 0.5 M H 2 SO 4 = 0.5 mol {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of moles present in 30 mL of 0.5 M {tex}{H_2}S{O_4} = \frac{{0.5 \times 30}}{{1000}}{/tex} mol = 0.015 mol Therefore, molarity {tex} = \frac{{0.015}}{{0.5\,L}}{/tex} mol = 0.03 M
solution_html:
Molarity is given by:
Molarity {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume solution in litre}}}}{/tex}

  1. Molar mass of Co(NO3)2 6H2O = 59 + 2 (14 + 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 16) + (6 {tex} \times {/tex}18)= 291 g mol-1
    Therefore, Moles of {tex}Co{(N{O_3})_2} \cdot 6{H_2}O{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{30}}{{291}}{/tex} mol
    = 0.103 mol
    Therefore, molarity {tex} = \frac{{0.103mol}}{{4.3L}}{/tex}
    = 0.023 M
  2. Number of moles present in 1000 mL of 0.5 M H2SO4 = 0.5 mol
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of moles present in 30 mL of 0.5 M {tex}{H_2}S{O_4} = \frac{{0.5 \times 30}}{{1000}}{/tex} mol
    = 0.015 mol
    Therefore, molarity{tex} = \frac{{0.015}}{{0.5\,L}}{/tex} mol
    = 0.03 M

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the mass of urea ( {tex}N{H_2}CON{H_2}{/tex} ) required in making 2.5 kg of 0.25 molal aqueous solution.
question_html: Calculate the mass of urea ({tex}N{H_2}CON{H_2}{/tex}) required in making 2.5 kg of 0.25 molal aqueous solution.
solution_text: Molar mass of urea {tex}N{H_2}CON{H_2} = 2(1 \times 14 + 2 \times 1) + 1 \times 12 + 1 \times 16 = 60 g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} 1000 g of water contains 0.25 mol = {tex}\left( {0.25 \times 60} \right)g{/tex} of urea = 15 g of urea That is, (1000 + 15) g of solution contains 15 g of urea Therefore, 2.5 kg (2500 g) of solution contains {tex} = \frac{{15 \times 2500}}{{1000 + 15}}g{/tex} = 36.95 g = 37 g of urea (approximately) Hence, mass of urea required = 37 g
solution_html:
Molar mass of urea {tex}N{H_2}CON{H_2} = 2(1 \times 14 + 2 \times 1) + 1 \times 12 + 1 \times 16 = 60 g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
1000 g of water contains 0.25 mol = {tex}\left( {0.25 \times 60} \right)g{/tex} of urea
= 15 g of urea
That is,
(1000 + 15) g of solution contains 15 g of urea
Therefore, 2.5 kg (2500 g) of solution contains {tex} = \frac{{15 \times 2500}}{{1000 + 15}}g{/tex}
= 36.95 g
= 37 g of urea (approximately)
Hence, mass of urea required = 37 g

question_html: Calculate (a) molality (b) molarity and (c) mole fraction of KI if the density of 20% (mass/mass) aqueous KI is 1.202 g mL-1.
solution_text: Molar mass of KI = 39 + 127 = 166 {tex}g{\rm{ \;}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} 20% (mass/mass) aqueous solution of KI means 20 g of KI is present in 100 g of solution. 1. Molar mass of KI = 39 + 127 = 166 {tex}g{\rm{ \;}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
    20% (mass/mass) aqueous solution of KI means 20 g of KI is present in 100 g of solution.

question_text: H 2 S, a toxic gas with rotten egg like smell, is used for the qualitative analysis. If the solubility of H 2 S in water at STP is 0.195 m, calculate Henry's law constant.
question_html: H2S, a toxic gas with rotten egg like smell, is used for the qualitative analysis. If the solubility of H2S in water at STP is 0.195 m, calculate Henry's law constant.
solution_text: It is given that the solubility of H 2 S in water at STP is 0.195 m, i.e., 0.195 mol of H 2 S is dissolved in 1000 g of water. Moles of water {tex} = \frac{{1000g}}{{18g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 55.56 mol ∴ Mole fraction of H 2 S, {tex}x = \frac{{{\text{Moles of }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}}}{{{\text{Moles of }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S + Moles of water}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.195}}{{0.195 + 55.56}}{/tex} = 0.0035 At STP, pressure ( p ) = 0.987 bar According to Henry's law: p = K H x {tex}{K_H} = \frac{P}{x}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{0.987}}{{0.0035}}bar{/tex} = 282 bar
solution_html:
It is given that the solubility of H2S in water at STP is 0.195 m, i.e., 0.195 mol of H2S is dissolved in 1000 g of water.
Moles of water {tex} = \frac{{1000g}}{{18g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}
= 55.56 mol
∴ Mole fraction of H2S, {tex}x = \frac{{{\text{Moles of }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}}}{{{\text{Moles of }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S + Moles of water}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.195}}{{0.195 + 55.56}}{/tex}
= 0.0035
At STP, pressure (p) = 0.987 bar
According to Henry's law: p= KHx
{tex}{K_H} = \frac{P}{x}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{0.987}}{{0.0035}}bar{/tex}
= 282 bar

question_text: Henry's law constant for CO 2 in water is 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 Pa at 298 K. Calculate the quantity of CO 2 in 500 mL of soda water when packed under 2.5 atm CO 2 pressure at 298 K.
question_html: Henry's law constant for CO2 in water is 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 Pa at 298 K. Calculate the quantity of CO2 in 500 mL of soda water when packed under 2.5 atm CO2  pressure at 298 K.
solution_text: It is given that: KH = {tex}1.67\times10^8Pa{/tex} {tex}{P_{c{o_2}}}{/tex} = 2.5 atm = {tex}2.5\times1.01325\times10^5Pa{/tex} {tex}=2.533125\times10^5Pa{/tex} According to Henry's law: {tex}P_{CO_2}=K_{H\;}\;x{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{Pc{o_2}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.533125 \times {{10}^5}}}{{1.67 \times {{10}^8}}}{/tex} = 0.00152 We can write, {tex}x = \frac{{{n_{co}}_{_2}}}{{{n_{c{o_2}}} + {n_{{H_2}O}}}} \approx \frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}}{/tex} [Since, {tex}{n_{c{o_2}}}{/tex} is negligible as compared to {tex}{n_{{H_2}O}}{/tex} ] In 500 mL of soda water, the volume of water = 500 mL [Neglecting the amount of soda present] We can write: 500 mL of water = 500 g of water = {tex}\frac{{500}}{{18}}{/tex} mol of water = 27.78 mol of water Now, {tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}} = x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{27.78}} = 0.00152{/tex} {tex}{n_{c{o_2}}} = 0.042\,mol{/tex} Hence, quantity of CO 2 in 500 mL of soda water = {tex}(0.042 \times 44)g{/tex} = 1.848 g
solution_html:
It is given that:
KH = {tex}1.67\times10^8Pa{/tex}
{tex}{P_{c{o_2}}}{/tex} = 2.5 atm = {tex}2.5\times1.01325\times10^5Pa{/tex}
{tex}=2.533125\times10^5Pa{/tex}
According to Henry's law:
{tex}P_{CO_2}=K_{H\;}\;x{/tex}
{tex}x = \frac{{Pc{o_2}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{2.533125 \times {{10}^5}}}{{1.67 \times {{10}^8}}}{/tex}
= 0.00152
We can write, {tex}x = \frac{{{n_{co}}_{_2}}}{{{n_{c{o_2}}} + {n_{{H_2}O}}}} \approx \frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}}{/tex}
[Since, {tex}{n_{c{o_2}}}{/tex}is negligible as compared to {tex}{n_{{H_2}O}}{/tex}]
In 500 mL of soda water, the volume of water = 500 mL
[Neglecting the amount of soda present]
We can write:
500 mL of water = 500 g of water
={tex}\frac{{500}}{{18}}{/tex} mol of water
= 27.78 mol of water
Now, {tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}} = x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{27.78}} = 0.00152{/tex}
{tex}{n_{c{o_2}}} = 0.042\,mol{/tex}
Hence, quantity of CO2 in 500 mL of soda water = {tex}(0.042 \times 44)g{/tex}
= 1.848 g

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The vapour pressure of pure liquids A and B are 450 and 700 mm Hg respectively, at 350 K. Find out the composition of the liquid mixture if total vapour pressure is 600 mm Hg. Also find the composition of the vapour phase.
question_html: The vapour pressure of pure liquids A and B are 450 and 700 mm Hg respectively, at 350 K. Find out the composition of the liquid mixture if total vapour pressure is 600 mm Hg. Also find the composition of the vapour phase.
solution_text: It is given that: {tex}P_A^0{/tex} = 450 mm of Hg {tex}P_B^0{/tex} = 700 mm of Hg P total = 600 mm of Hg From Raoult's law, we have: {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B} = p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex} Therefore, total pressure, {tex}{p_{total}} = {p_A} + {p_B}{/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0 - p_B^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = \left( {p_A^0 - p_B^0} \right){x_A} + p_B^0{/tex} {tex}600 = (450 - 700) {x_A} + 700{/tex} {tex} - 100 = - 250{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{x_A} = 0.4{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{x_B} = 1 - {x_A}{/tex} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 Now, {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}= 450 \times 0.4{/tex} = 180 mm of Hg {tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B}{/tex} {tex} = 700 \times 0.6{/tex} = 420 mm of Hg Now, in the vapour phase: Mole fraction of liquid {tex}A = \frac{{{p_A}}}{{{p_A} + {p_B}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{180}}{{180 + 420}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{180}}{{600}}{/tex} = 0.30 And, mole fraction of liquid B = 1 - 0.30
solution_html:
It is given that:
{tex}P_A^0{/tex} = 450 mm of Hg
{tex}P_B^0{/tex} = 700 mm of Hg
Ptotal = 600 mm of Hg
From Raoult's law, we have:
{tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex}
{tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B} = p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex}
Therefore, total pressure, {tex}{p_{total}} = {p_A} + {p_B}{/tex}
{tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex}
{tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0 - p_B^0{x_A}{/tex}
{tex}{p_{total}} = \left( {p_A^0 - p_B^0} \right){x_A} + p_B^0{/tex}
{tex}600 = (450 - 700) {x_A} + 700{/tex}
{tex} - 100 = - 250{x_A}{/tex}
{tex}{x_A} = 0.4{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}{x_B} = 1 - {x_A}{/tex}
= 1 - 0.4 = 0.6
Now, {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex}
{tex}= 450 \times 0.4{/tex}
= 180 mm of Hg
{tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B}{/tex}
{tex} = 700 \times 0.6{/tex}
= 420 mm of Hg
Now, in the vapour phase:
Mole fraction of liquid {tex}A = \frac{{{p_A}}}{{{p_A} + {p_B}}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{180}}{{180 + 420}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{180}}{{600}}{/tex} = 0.30
And, mole fraction of liquid B = 1 - 0.30

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Vapour pressure of pure water at 298 K is 23.8 mm Hg. 50 g of urea (NH 2 CONH 2 ) is dissolved in 850 g of water. Calculate the vapour pressure of water for this solution and its relative lowering.
question_html: Vapour pressure of pure water at 298 K is 23.8 mm Hg. 50 g of urea (NH2CONH2) is dissolved in 850 g of water. Calculate the vapour pressure of water for this solution and its relative lowering.
solution_text: It is given that vapour pressure of water, {tex}p_1^0 = 23.8 mm{/tex} of Hg Weight of water taken, {tex}{w_1} = 850{\rm{ }}g{/tex} Weight of urea taken, {tex}{w_2} = 50{\rm{ }}g{/tex} Molecular weight of water, {tex} {M_1} = 18{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Molecular weight of urea, {tex}{M_2} = 60g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Now, we have to calculate vapour pressure of water in the solution. We take vapour pressure as p1. Now, from Raoult's law, we have: {tex}\frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1} + {n_2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{\frac{{{w_2}}}{{{M_2}}}}}{{\frac{{{w_1}}}{{{M_1}}} + \frac{{{w_2}}}{{{M_2}}}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{23.8 - {p_1}}}{{23.8}} = \frac{{\frac{{50}}{{60}}}}{{\frac{{850}}{{18}} + \frac{{50}}{{60}}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{23.8 - {p_1}}}{{23.8}} = \frac{{0.83}}{{47.22 + 0.83}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{23.8 - {p_1}}}{{23.8}} = 0.0173; {p_1} = 23.38mm\,of\,Hg{/tex} Hence, the vapour pressure of water in the given solution is 23.4 mm of Hg and its relative lowering is 0.0173.
solution_html:
It is given that vapour pressure of water, {tex}p_1^0 = 23.8 mm{/tex} of Hg Weight of water taken, {tex}{w_1} = 850{\rm{ }}g{/tex}
Weight of urea taken, {tex}{w_2} = 50{\rm{ }}g{/tex}
Molecular weight of water,{tex} {M_1} = 18{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Molecular weight of urea, {tex}{M_2} = 60g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Now, we have to calculate vapour pressure of water in the solution. We take vapour pressure as p1.
Now, from Raoult's law, we have:
{tex}\frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1} + {n_2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{\frac{{{w_2}}}{{{M_2}}}}}{{\frac{{{w_1}}}{{{M_1}}} + \frac{{{w_2}}}{{{M_2}}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{23.8 - {p_1}}}{{23.8}} = \frac{{\frac{{50}}{{60}}}}{{\frac{{850}}{{18}} + \frac{{50}}{{60}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{23.8 - {p_1}}}{{23.8}} = \frac{{0.83}}{{47.22 + 0.83}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{23.8 - {p_1}}}{{23.8}} = 0.0173; {p_1} = 23.38mm\,of\,Hg{/tex}
Hence, the vapour pressure of water in the given solution is 23.4 mm of Hg and its relative lowering is 0.0173.

question_text: Boiling point of water at 750 mm Hg is 99.63 o C. How much sucrose is to be added to 500 g of water such that it boils at 100 o C.
question_html: Boiling point of water at 750 mm Hg is 99.63oC. How much sucrose is to be added to 500 g of water such that it boils at 100oC.
solution_text: Here, elevation of boiling point {tex}\Delta {T_b}{/tex} = (100 + 273) - (99.63 + 273) = 0.37 K Mass of water, {tex}{w_1} = {\rm{ }}500{\rm{ }}g{/tex} Molar mass of sucrose ( {tex}{C_{12}}{H_{22}}{O_{11}}{/tex} ), {tex}{M_2} = (12 \times 12 )+( 22 \times 1 )+ (11 \times 16){/tex} {tex}= 342{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Molal elevation constant, {tex}K_b = 0.52{\rm{ }}K{\rm{ }}\ kg{\rm{ }}\ mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} We know that: {tex}\Delta {T_b} = \frac{{{K_b} \times 1000 \times {w_2}}}{{{M_2} \times {w_1}}}{/tex} {tex}{w_2} = \frac{{\Delta {T_b} \times {M_2} \times {w_1}}}{{{K_b} \times 1000}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.37 \times 342 \times 500}}{{0.52 \times 1000}}{/tex} = 121.67 g (approx) Hence, 121.67 g of sucrose is to be added to 500 g of water such that it boils at {tex}{100^0}C{/tex} .
solution_html:
Here, elevation of boiling point {tex}\Delta {T_b}{/tex} = (100 + 273) - (99.63 + 273)
= 0.37 K
Mass of water, {tex}{w_1} = {\rm{ }}500{\rm{ }}g{/tex}
Molar mass of sucrose ({tex}{C_{12}}{H_{22}}{O_{11}}{/tex}), {tex}{M_2} = (12 \times 12 )+( 22 \times 1 )+ (11 \times 16){/tex} {tex}= 342{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Molal elevation constant, {tex}K_b = 0.52{\rm{ }}K{\rm{ }}\ kg{\rm{ }}\ mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
We know that:
{tex}\Delta {T_b} = \frac{{{K_b} \times 1000 \times {w_2}}}{{{M_2} \times {w_1}}}{/tex}
{tex}{w_2} = \frac{{\Delta {T_b} \times {M_2} \times {w_1}}}{{{K_b} \times 1000}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.37 \times 342 \times 500}}{{0.52 \times 1000}}{/tex}
= 121.67 g (approx)
Hence, 121.67 g of sucrose is to be added to 500 g of water such that it boils at {tex}{100^0}C{/tex}.

question_text: Calculate the mass of ascorbic acid (Vitamin C, C 6 H 8 O 6 ) to be dissolved in 75 g of acetic acid to lower its melting point by 1.5 o C. K f = 3.9 K kg mol -1 .
question_html: Calculate the mass of ascorbic acid (Vitamin C, C6H8O6) to be dissolved in 75 g of acetic acid to lower its melting point by 1.5oC. Kf = 3.9 K kg mol-1.
solution_text: Mass of acetic acid, w 1 = 75 Molar mass of ascorbic acid (C 6 H 8 O 5 ), M 2 = (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) +( 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 ) + (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 16) = 176 g mol -1 Lowering of melting point, {tex}\Delta T{\text{f}} = 1.5\,K{/tex} We know that: {tex}\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times {w_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {w_1}}}{/tex} {tex}{w_2} = \frac{{\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} \times {M_2} \times {w_1}}}{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times 1000}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.5 \times 176 \times 75}}{{3.9 \times 1000}}{/tex} = 5.076 g Hence, 5.076 g of ascorbic acid is needed to be dissolved.
solution_html:
Mass of acetic acid, w1 = 75
Molar mass of ascorbic acid (C6H8O5), M2 = (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) +( 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 ) + (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 16)
= 176 g mol-1
Lowering of melting point, {tex}\Delta T{\text{f}} = 1.5\,K{/tex}

{tex}{w_2} = \frac{{\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} \times {M_2} \times {w_1}}}{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times 1000}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1.5 \times 176 \times 75}}{{3.9 \times 1000}}{/tex}
= 5.076 g 
Hence, 5.076 g of ascorbic acid is needed to be dissolved.

question_text: Calculate the osmotic pressure in pascals exerted by a solution prepared by dissolving 1.0 g of polymer of molar mass 185,000 in 450 mL of water at 37°C.
question_html: Calculate the osmotic pressure in pascals exerted by a solution prepared by dissolving 1.0 g of polymer of molar mass 185,000 in 450 mL of water at 37°C.
solution_text: It is given that: Volume of water, V = 450 mL = 0.45 L Temperature, T = (37 + 273) K = 310 K Number of moles of the polymer, {tex}n = \frac{1}{{185000}}mol{/tex} We know that: Osmotic pressure, {tex}\pi = \frac{n}{V}RT{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{185000}}mol \times \frac{1}{{0.45}} \times 8.314 \times {10^3}PaL{K^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 310K{/tex} = 30.98 Pa = 31 Pa (approximately)

= 30.98 Pa

question_text: Define the term solution. How many types of solutions are formed? Write briefly about each type with an example.
question_html: Define the term solution. How many types of solutions are formed? Write briefly about each type with an example.
solution_text: Homogeneous mixtures of two or more than two components are known as solutions. There are three types of solutions. Gaseous solution: The solution in which the solvent is a gas is called a gaseous solution. In these solutions, the solute may be liquid, solid, or gas. For example, a mixture of oxygen and nitrogen gas is a gaseous solution. Liquid solution: The solution in which the solvent is a liquid is known as a liquid solution. The solute in these solutions may be gas, liquid, or solid. For example, a solution of ethanol in water is a liquid solution. Solid solution: The solution in which the solvent is a solid is known as a solid solution. The solute may be gas, liquid or solid. For example, a solution of copper in gold is a solid solution.
solution_html:
Homogeneous mixtures of two or more than two components are known as solutions.
There are three types of solutions.

  1. Gaseous solution: The solution in which the solvent is a gas is called a gaseous solution. In these solutions, the solute may be liquid, solid, or gas. For example, a mixture of oxygen and nitrogen gas is a gaseous solution.
  2. Liquid solution: The solution in which the solvent is a liquid is known as a liquid solution. The solute in these solutions may be gas, liquid, or solid. For example, a solution of ethanol in water is a liquid solution.
  3. Solid solution: The solution in which the solvent is a solid is known as a solid solution. The solute may be gas, liquid or solid. For example, a solution of copper in gold is a solid solution.

question_text: Give an example of solid solution in which the solute is a gas.
question_html: Give an example of solid solution in which the solute is a gas.
solution_text: In case a solid solution is formed between two substances (one having very large particles and the other having very small particles), an interstitial solid solution will be formed. For example, a solution of hydrogen in palladium is a solid solution in which the solute is a gas.
solution_html: In case a solid solution is formed between two substances (one having very large particles and the other having very small particles), an interstitial solid solution will be formed. For example, a solution of hydrogen in palladium is a solid solution in which the solute is a gas.

question_text: Define the following terms: Mole fraction Molality Molarity Mass percentage.
question_html:
Define the following terms:

  1. Mole fraction
  2. Molality
  3. Molarity
  4. Mass percentage.
solution_text: Mole fraction: The mole fraction of a component in a mixture is defined as the ratio of the number of moles of the component to the total number of moles of all the components in the mixture. i.e., Mole fraction of a component = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Number \;of \;moles\; of\; the\; component}}}}{{{\rm{Total \;number\; of \;moles \;of \;all\; components}}}}{/tex} Mole fraction is denoted by 'x'. If in a binary solution, the number of moles of the solute and the solvent are nA and nB respectively, then the mole fraction of the solute in the solution is given by, {tex}{x_A} = \frac{{{n_A}}}{{{n_A} + {n_B}}}{/tex} Similarly, the mole fraction of the solvent in the solution is given as: {tex}{x_B} = \frac{{{n_B}}}{{{n_A} + {n_B}}}{/tex} Molality Molality (m) is defined as the number of moles of the solute dissolved per kilogram of the solvent. It is expressed as: Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Moles\; of\; solute}}}}{{{\rm{Mass \;of \;solvent\; in \;kg}}}}{/tex} It is expressed as mol/Kg Molarity Molarity (M) is defined as the number of moles of the solute dissolved in one Litre of the solution. It is expressed as: Molarity (M) = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Moles\; of \;solute}}}}{{{\rm{Volume \;of \;solvent \;in \;Litre}}}}{/tex} It is expressed as mol/L Mass percentage: The mass percentage of a component of a solution is defined as the mass of the solute in grams present in 100 g of the solution. It is expressed as: Mass % of a component = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Mass\; of\; component\; in \;solution}}}}{{{\rm{Total\; mass\; of \;solution}}}} \times 100{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Mole fraction:
    The mole fraction of a component in a mixture is defined as the ratio of the number of moles of the component to the total number of moles of all the components in the mixture.
    i.e., Mole fraction of a component = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Number \;of \;moles\; of\; the\; component}}}}{{{\rm{Total \;number\; of \;moles \;of \;all\; components}}}}{/tex}
    Mole fraction is denoted by 'x'.
    If in a binary solution, the number of moles of the solute and the solvent are nA and nB respectively, then the mole fraction of the solute in the solution is given by,
    {tex}{x_A} = \frac{{{n_A}}}{{{n_A} + {n_B}}}{/tex}
    Similarly, the mole fraction of the solvent in the solution is given as:
    {tex}{x_B} = \frac{{{n_B}}}{{{n_A} + {n_B}}}{/tex}
  2. Molality
    Molality (m) is defined as the number of moles of the solute dissolved per kilogram of the solvent. It is expressed as:
    Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Moles\; of\; solute}}}}{{{\rm{Mass \;of \;solvent\; in \;kg}}}}{/tex}  It is expressed as mol/Kg
  3. Molarity
    Molarity (M) is defined as the number of moles of the solute dissolved in one Litre of the solution.
    It is expressed as:
    Molarity (M) = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Moles\; of \;solute}}}}{{{\rm{Volume \;of \;solvent \;in \;Litre}}}}{/tex} It is expressed as mol/L
  4. Mass percentage:
    The mass percentage of a component of a solution is defined as the mass of the solute in grams present in 100 g of the solution. It is expressed as:
    Mass % of a component = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Mass\; of\; component\; in \;solution}}}}{{{\rm{Total\; mass\; of \;solution}}}} \times 100{/tex}

question_text: Concentrated nitric acid used in laboratory work is 68% nitric acid by mass in aqueous solution. What should be the molarity of such a sample of the acid if the density of the solution is 1.504 g mL -1 ?
question_html: Concentrated nitric acid used in laboratory work is 68% nitric acid by mass in aqueous solution. What should be the molarity of such a sample of the acid if the density of the solution is 1.504 g mL-1?
solution_text: Molarity of solution = {tex}\frac{{1.079\,mol}}{{66.49 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}L}}{/tex} =16.23 M Concentrated nitric acid used in laboratory work is 68% nitric acid by mass in an aqueous solution. This means that 68 g of nitric acid is dissolved in 100 g of the solution. Molar mass of nitric acid {tex}\left( {HN{O_3}} \right) = 1 \times 1 + 1 \times 14 + 3 \times 16 = 63{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Then, number of moles {tex}HN{O_3} = \frac{{68}}{{63}}mol{/tex} = 1.079 mol Given, Density of solution {tex}= 1.504{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}m{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} Therefore, Volume of 100 g solution = {tex}\frac{{100}}{{1.504}}mL{/tex} = 66.49 mL = {tex}66.49 \times {10^{ - 3}}L{/tex} Molarity of solution = {tex}\frac{{1.079\,mol}}{{66.49 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}L}}{/tex} = 16.23 M Alternatively molarity can be calculated by the formula M = % × d × 10/mol mass of solute = 68x 1.504 × 10/63 = 16.233M

=16.23 M

question_text: A solution of glucose in water is labelled as 10% w/w, what would be the molality and mole fraction of each component in the solution? If the density of solution is {tex}1.2\,g\,m{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} , then what shall be the molarity of the solution?
question_html: A solution of glucose in water is labelled as 10% w/w, what would be the molality and mole fraction of each component in the solution? If the density of solution is {tex}1.2\,g\,m{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}, then what shall be the molarity of the solution?
solution_text: 10% w/w solution of glucose in water means that 10 g of glucose in present in 100 g of the solution i.e., 10 g of glucose is present in (100 - 10) g = 90 g of water. Molar mass of glucose {tex}\left( {{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}} \right) = (6 \times 12 )+( 12 \times 1) +( 6 \times 16 )= 180{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Then, number of moles of glucose = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{180}}mol{/tex} = 0.056 mol Molality of solution = {tex}\frac{{0.056\,mol}}{{0.09\,kg}}{/tex} = 0.62 Number of moles of water = {tex}\frac{{90\,g}}{{18\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 5 mol Mole fraction of glucose {tex}({x_g}) = \frac{{0.056}}{{0.056 + 5}}{/tex} = 0.011 And, mole fraction of water {tex}{x_w} = 1 - {x_g}{/tex} = 1 - 0.011 = 0.989 If the density of the solution is {tex}1.2\,g\,m{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} , then the volume of the 100 g solution can be given as: {tex} = \frac{{100\,g}}{{1.2\,g\,m{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 83.33 mL {tex} = 83.33 \times {10^{ - 3}}L{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Molarity of the solution = {tex}\frac{{0.056\,mol}}{{83.33 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}L}}{/tex} =0.67 M
solution_html:
10% w/w solution of glucose in water means that 10 g of glucose in present in 100 g of the solution i.e., 10 g of glucose is present in (100 - 10) g = 90 g of water.
Molar mass of glucose {tex}\left( {{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}} \right) = (6 \times 12 )+( 12 \times 1) +( 6 \times 16 )= 180{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Then, number of moles of glucose = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{180}}mol{/tex}
= 0.056 mol
Molality of solution = {tex}\frac{{0.056\,mol}}{{0.09\,kg}}{/tex} = 0.62
Number of moles of water = {tex}\frac{{90\,g}}{{18\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}
= 5 mol
Mole fraction of glucose {tex}({x_g}) = \frac{{0.056}}{{0.056 + 5}}{/tex}
= 0.011
And, mole fraction of water {tex}{x_w} = 1 - {x_g}{/tex}
= 1 - 0.011
= 0.989
If the density of the solution is {tex}1.2\,g\,m{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}, then the volume of the 100 g solution can be given as:
{tex} = \frac{{100\,g}}{{1.2\,g\,m{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}
= 83.33 mL
{tex} = 83.33 \times {10^{ - 3}}L{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Molarity of the solution = {tex}\frac{{0.056\,mol}}{{83.33 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}L}}{/tex}
=0.67 M

question_text: How many mL of 0.1 M HCl are required to react completely with 1g mixture of Na 2 CO 3 and NaHCO 3 containing equimolar amounts of both?
question_html: How many mL of 0.1 M HCl are required to react completely with 1g mixture of Na2CO3 and NaHCO3 containing equimolar amounts of both?
solution_text: Let the amount of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} in the mixture be x g. Then, the amount of {tex}NaHC{O_3}{/tex} in the mixture is (1-x)g Molar mass of {tex} N{a_2}C{O_3} =( 2 \times 23) +( 1 \times 12) + (3 \times 16){/tex} = 106 gmo -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of moles {tex} N{a_2}C{O_3} = \frac{x}{{106}}mol{/tex} Molar mass of {tex}NaHC{O_3} = (1 \times 23) +( 1 \times 1) + (1\times12) + (3 \times 16){/tex} = {tex}84{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Number of moles of {tex}NaHC{O_3} = \frac{{1 - x}}{{84}}mol{/tex} According to the question, {tex}\frac{x}{{106}} = \frac{{1 - x}}{{84}} \Rightarrow 84 x = 106 - 106x \Rightarrow 190 x = 106{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x = 0.5579 Therefore, number of moles of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3} = \frac{{0.5579}}{{106}}mol{/tex} = 0.0053 mol And, number of moles of {tex}NaHC{O_3} = \frac{{1 - 0.5579}}{{84}}{/tex} = 0.0053 mol HCl reacts with {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3} \ and\ NaHC{O_3}{/tex} according to the following equation. {tex}\mathop {2HCl}\limits_{2\,mol} + \mathop {N{a_2}C{O_3}}\limits_{1\,mol} \to 2NaCl + {H_2}O + C{O_2}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {HCl}\limits_{1\,mol} + \mathop {NaHC{O_3}}\limits_{1\,mol} \to NaCl + {H_2}O + C{O_2}{/tex} 1 mol of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} reacts with 2 mol of HCl. Therefore, 0.0053 mol of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} reacts with 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0053 mol = 0.0106 mol. Similarly, 1 mol of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} reacts with 1 mol of HCl Therefore, 0.0053 mol of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} reacts with 0.0053 mol of HCl. Total moles of HCl required = (0.0106 + 0.0053) mol = 0.0159 mol In 0.1 M of HCl, 0.1 mol of HCl is present in 1000 mL of the solution. Therefore, 0.0159 mol of HCl is present in {tex}\frac{{1000 \times 0.0159}}{{0.1}}mol{/tex} = 159 mL of the solution Hence, 159 mL of 0.1 M of HCl is required to react completely with 1g mixture of Na 2 CO 3 and NaHCO 3 containing equimolar amounts of both.
solution_html:
Let the amount of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} in the mixture be x g.
Then, the amount of {tex}NaHC{O_3}{/tex} in the mixture is (1-x)g
Molar mass of {tex} N{a_2}C{O_3} =( 2 \times 23) +( 1 \times 12) + (3 \times 16){/tex}
= 106 gmo-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of moles {tex} N{a_2}C{O_3} = \frac{x}{{106}}mol{/tex}
Molar mass of {tex}NaHC{O_3} = (1 \times 23) +( 1 \times 1) + (1\times12) + (3 \times 16){/tex}
= {tex}84{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Number of moles of {tex}NaHC{O_3} = \frac{{1 - x}}{{84}}mol{/tex}
According to the question,
{tex}\frac{x}{{106}} = \frac{{1 - x}}{{84}} \Rightarrow 84 x = 106 - 106x \Rightarrow 190 x = 106{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x = 0.5579
Therefore, number of moles of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3} = \frac{{0.5579}}{{106}}mol{/tex}
= 0.0053 mol
And, number of moles of {tex}NaHC{O_3} = \frac{{1 - 0.5579}}{{84}}{/tex}
= 0.0053 mol
HCl reacts with {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3} \ and\ NaHC{O_3}{/tex} according to the following equation.
{tex}\mathop {2HCl}\limits_{2\,mol} + \mathop {N{a_2}C{O_3}}\limits_{1\,mol} \to 2NaCl + {H_2}O + C{O_2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {HCl}\limits_{1\,mol} + \mathop {NaHC{O_3}}\limits_{1\,mol} \to NaCl + {H_2}O + C{O_2}{/tex}
1 mol of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} reacts with 2 mol of HCl.
Therefore, 0.0053 mol of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} reacts with 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0053 mol = 0.0106 mol.
Similarly, 1 mol of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} reacts with 1 mol of HCl
Therefore, 0.0053 mol of {tex}N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex} reacts with 0.0053 mol of HCl.
Total moles of HCl required = (0.0106 + 0.0053) mol
= 0.0159 mol
In 0.1 M of HCl,
0.1 mol of HCl is present in 1000 mL of the solution.
Therefore, 0.0159 mol of HCl is present in {tex}\frac{{1000 \times 0.0159}}{{0.1}}mol{/tex}
= 159 mL of the solution
Hence, 159 mL of 0.1 M of HCl is required to react completely with 1g mixture of Na2CO3 and NaHCO3 containing equimolar amounts of both.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What role does the molecular interaction play in a solution of alcohol and water?
question_html: What role does the molecular interaction play in a solution of alcohol and water?
solution_text: In pure alcohol and water, the molecules are held tightly by a strong hydrogen bonding. The interaction between the molecules of alcohol and water is weaker than alcohol-alcohol and water-water interactions. As a result, when alcohol and water are mixed, the inter-molecular interactions become weaker and the molecules can easily escape. This increases the vapour pressure of the solution, which in turn lowers the boiling point of the resulting solution.

The interaction between the molecules of alcohol and water is weaker than alcohol-alcohol and water-water interactions. As a result, when alcohol and water are mixed, the inter-molecular interactions become weaker and the molecules can easily escape. This increases the vapour pressure of the solution, which in turn lowers the boiling point of the resulting solution.

question_text: Why do gases always tend to be less soluble in liquids as the temperature is raised?
question_html: Why do gases always tend to be less soluble in liquids as the temperature is raised?
solution_text: Mostly dissolution of gases in liquid is an exothermic process. It is because the fact that this process involves decrease of entropy. Thus, increase of temperature tends to push the equilibrium in backward direction as a result solubility of the gas decreases. (Gas + Solvent {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Solution + Heat)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: State Henry's law and mention some important applications?
question_html: State Henry's law and mention some important applications?
solution_text: Henry's law states that partial pressure of a gas in the vapour phase is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas dissolved in the solution. If p is the partial pressure of the gas in the vapour phase and x is the mole fraction of the gas, then Henry's law can be expressed as: {tex}p = {K_H}x{/tex} Where, {tex}{K_H}{/tex} is Henry's law constant Some important applications of Henry's law are mentioned below. Bottles are sealed under high pressure to increase the solubility of {tex}C{O_2}{/tex} in soft drinks and soda water. Henry's law states that the solubility of gases increases with an increase in pressure. Therefore, when a scuba diver dives deep into the sea, the increased sea pressure causes the nitrogen present in air to dissolve in his blood in great amounts. As a result, when he comes back to the surface, the solubility of nitrogen again decreases and the dissolved gas is released, leading to the formation of nitrogen bubbles in the blood. This results in the blockage of capillaries and leads to a medical condition known as 'bends' or 'decompression sickness'. Hence, the oxygen tanks used by scuba divers are filled with air and diluted with helium to avoid bends. The concentration of oxygen is low in the blood and tissues of people living at high altitudes such as climbers. This is because at high altitudes, partial pressure of oxygen is less than that at ground level. Low-blood oxygen causes climbers to become weak and disables them from thinking clearly. These are symptoms of anoxia.
solution_html:
Henry's law states that partial pressure of a gas in the vapour phase is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas dissolved in the solution. If p is the partial pressure of the gas in the vapour phase and x is the mole fraction of the gas, then Henry's law can be expressed as:
{tex}p = {K_H}x{/tex}
Where, {tex}{K_H}{/tex} is Henry's law constant
Some important applications of Henry's law are mentioned below.

  1. Bottles are sealed under high pressure to increase the solubility of {tex}C{O_2}{/tex} in soft drinks and soda water.
  2. Henry's law states that the solubility of gases increases with an increase in pressure. Therefore, when a scuba diver dives deep into the sea, the increased sea pressure causes the nitrogen present in air to dissolve in his blood in great amounts. As a result, when he comes back to the surface, the solubility of nitrogen again decreases and the dissolved gas is released, leading to the formation of nitrogen bubbles in the blood. This results in the blockage of capillaries and leads to a medical condition known as 'bends' or 'decompression sickness'.
    Hence, the oxygen tanks used by scuba divers are filled with air and diluted with helium to avoid bends.
  3. The concentration of oxygen is low in the blood and tissues of people living at high altitudes such as climbers. This is because at high altitudes, partial pressure of oxygen is less than that at ground level. Low-blood oxygen causes climbers to become weak and disables them from thinking clearly. These are symptoms of anoxia.

question_text: The partial pressure of ethane over a solution containing 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 of ethane is 1 bar. If the solution contains 5.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g of ethane, then what shall be the partial pressure of the gas?
question_html: The partial pressure of ethane over a solution containing 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 of ethane is 1 bar. If the solution contains 5.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g of ethane, then what shall be the partial pressure of the gas?
solution_text: Applying the relationship m = K H p In the first case, 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g = K H {tex}\times{/tex} 1 bar K H = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g/bar In second case, 5.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g/bar {tex}\times{/tex} p P = {tex}\frac{{5.00 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g}}{{6.56 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g\,ba{r^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.7621 bar
solution_html:
Applying the relationship
m = KHp

In second case,

P = {tex}\frac{{5.00 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g}}{{6.56 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g\,ba{r^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.7621 bar

question_text: What is meant by positive and negative deviations from Raoult's law and how is the sign of {tex}\Delta{/tex} mix H related to positive and negative deviations from Raoult's law?
question_html: What is meant by positive and negative deviations from Raoult's law and how is the sign of  {tex}\Delta{/tex}mixH related to positive and negative deviations from Raoult's law?
solution_text: According to Raoult's law, the partial vapour pressure of each volatile component in any solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction. The solutions which obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration are known as ideal solutions. The solutions that do not obey Raoult's law (non-ideal solutions) have vapour pressures either higher or lower than that predicted by Raoult's law. If the vapour pressure is higher,(ie A....B interaction is less than A...A and B...B interaction) then the solution is said to exhibit positive deviation, and if it is lower(A...B interaction is more than A...A and B...B interaction), then the solution is said to exhibit negative deviation from Raoult's law. Vapour pressure of a two-component solution showing positive deviation from Raoult's law Vapour pressure of a two-component solution showing negative deviation from Raoult's law. In the case of an ideal solution, the enthalpy of the mixing of the pure components for forming the solution is zero. {tex}\Delta{/tex} Sol H = 0 In the case of solutions showing positive deviations, absorption of heat takes place. Therefore, {tex}\Delta{/tex} Sol H = Positive In the case of solutions showing negative deviations, evolution of heat takes place. Therefore, {tex}\Delta{/tex} Sol H = Negative

If the vapour pressure is higher,(ie A....B interaction is less than A...A and B...B interaction) then the solution is said to exhibit positive deviation, and if it is lower(A...B interaction is more than A...A and B...B interaction), then the solution is said to exhibit negative deviation from Raoult's law.

Vapour pressure of a two-component solution showing positive deviation from Raoult's law

Vapour pressure of a two-component solution showing negative deviation from Raoult's law.
In the case of an ideal solution, the enthalpy of the mixing of the pure components for forming the solution is zero.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Sol H = 0

Therefore, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Sol H = Positive
In the case of solutions showing negative deviations, evolution of heat takes place.
Therefore, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Sol H = Negative

subject: Chemistry
question_text: An aqueous solution of 2% non-volatile solute exerts a pressure of 1.004 bar at the normal boiling point of the solvent. What is the molar mass of the solute?
question_html: An aqueous solution of 2% non-volatile solute exerts a pressure of 1.004 bar at the normal boiling point of the solvent. What is the molar mass of the solute?
solution_text: Vapour pressure of the solution at normal boiling point ( {tex}p_1{/tex} ) = 1.004 bar Vapour pressure of pure water at normal boiling point {tex}\left( {p_1^0} \right) = 1.013\,bar{/tex} Mass of solute, {tex}(w_2{/tex} ) = 2 g Mass of solvent (water), ( {tex}w_1{/tex} ) = 98 g Molar mass of solvent (water), ( {tex}M_1{/tex} ) = {tex}18{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} According to Raoult's law, {tex}\frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{{w_2} \times {M_1}}}{{{M_2} \times {w_1}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{1.013 - 1.004}}{{1.013}} = \frac{{2 \times 18}}{{{M_2} \times 98}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{0.009}}{{1.013}} = \frac{{2 \times 18}}{{{M_2} \times 98}}{/tex} {tex}{M_2} = \frac{{1.013 \times 2 \times 18}}{{0.009 \times 98}}{/tex} = {tex}41.346{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Hence, the molar mass of the solute is {tex} 41.346{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Vapour pressure of the solution at normal boiling point ({tex}p_1{/tex}) = 1.004 bar
Vapour pressure of pure water at normal boiling point {tex}\left( {p_1^0} \right) = 1.013\,bar{/tex}
Mass of solute, {tex}(w_2{/tex}) = 2 g
Mass of solvent (water), ({tex}w_1{/tex}) = 98 g
Molar mass of solvent (water), ({tex}M_1{/tex}) = {tex}18{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
According to Raoult's law,
{tex}\frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{{w_2} \times {M_1}}}{{{M_2} \times {w_1}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{1.013 - 1.004}}{{1.013}} = \frac{{2 \times 18}}{{{M_2} \times 98}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{0.009}}{{1.013}} = \frac{{2 \times 18}}{{{M_2} \times 98}}{/tex}
{tex}{M_2} = \frac{{1.013 \times 2 \times 18}}{{0.009 \times 98}}{/tex}
= {tex}41.346{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Hence, the molar mass of the solute is {tex} 41.346{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Heptane and octane form an ideal solution. At 373 K, the vapour pressures of the two liquid components are 105.2 kPa and 46.8 kPa respectively. What will be the vapour pressure of a mixture of 26.0 g of heptane and 35 g of octane?
question_html: Heptane and octane form an ideal solution. At 373 K, the vapour pressures of the two liquid components are 105.2 kPa and 46.8 kPa respectively. What will be the vapour pressure of a mixture of 26.0 g of heptane and 35 g of octane?
solution_text: Vapour pressure of heptanes {tex}\left( {p_1^0} \right) = 105.2\,kPa{/tex} Vapour pressure of octane {tex}\left( {p_2^0} \right){/tex} = 46.8 kPa We know that, Molar mass of heptane {tex}\left( {{C_7}{H_{16}}} \right) =( 7 \times 12 )+( 16 \times 1){/tex} = {tex}100{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Therefore, Number of moles of heptane = {tex}\frac{{26}}{{100}}mol{/tex} = 0.26 mol Molar mass of octane {tex}\left( {{C_8}{H_{18}}} \right) = (8 \times 12 )+ (18 \times 1){/tex} = {tex}114{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Therefore, Number of moles of octane = {tex}\frac{{35}}{{114}}mol{/tex} = 0.31 mol Mole fraction of heptane, {tex}{x_1} = \frac{{0.26}}{{0.26 + 0.31}}{/tex} = 0.456 And, mole fraction of octane, {tex}{x_2}{/tex} = 1 - 0.456 = 0.544 Now, partial pressure of heptane, {tex}{p_1} = {x_1}p_1^0{/tex} = {tex}0.456 \times 105.2{/tex} = 47.97 kPa Partial pressure of octane, {tex}{p_2} = {x_2}p_2^0{/tex} = {tex}0.544 \times 46.8{/tex} = 25.46 kPa Hence, vapour pressure of solution, {tex}{p_{total}}{\rm{ }} = {p_1} + {p_2}{/tex} = 47.97 + 25.46 = 73.43 kPa
solution_html:
Vapour pressure of heptanes {tex}\left( {p_1^0} \right) = 105.2\,kPa{/tex}
Vapour pressure of octane {tex}\left( {p_2^0} \right){/tex} = 46.8 kPa
We know that,
Molar mass of heptane {tex}\left( {{C_7}{H_{16}}} \right) =( 7 \times 12 )+( 16 \times 1){/tex}
= {tex}100{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Therefore, Number of moles of heptane = {tex}\frac{{26}}{{100}}mol{/tex}
= 0.26 mol
Molar mass of octane {tex}\left( {{C_8}{H_{18}}} \right) = (8 \times 12 )+ (18 \times 1){/tex}
= {tex}114{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Therefore, Number of moles of octane = {tex}\frac{{35}}{{114}}mol{/tex}
= 0.31 mol
Mole fraction of heptane, {tex}{x_1} = \frac{{0.26}}{{0.26 + 0.31}}{/tex}
= 0.456
And, mole fraction of octane, {tex}{x_2}{/tex} = 1 - 0.456
= 0.544
Now, partial pressure of heptane, {tex}{p_1} = {x_1}p_1^0{/tex}
= {tex}0.456 \times 105.2{/tex}
= 47.97 kPa
Partial pressure of octane, {tex}{p_2} = {x_2}p_2^0{/tex}
= {tex}0.544 \times 46.8{/tex}
= 25.46 kPa
Hence, vapour pressure of solution, {tex}{p_{total}}{\rm{ }} = {p_1} + {p_2}{/tex}
= 47.97 + 25.46
= 73.43 kPa

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The vapour pressure of water is 12.3 kPa at 300 K. Calculate vapour pressure of 1 molal solution of a non-volatile solute in it.
question_html: The vapour pressure of water is 12.3 kPa at 300 K. Calculate vapour pressure of 1 molal solution of a non-volatile solute in it.
solution_text: 1 molal solution means 1 mol of the solute is present in 1000 g of the solvent (water). Molar mass of water = {tex}18{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Therefore, Number of moles present in 1000 g of water = {tex}\frac{{1000}}{{18}}{/tex} = 55.56 mol Therefore, mole fraction of the solute in the solution is {tex}{x_2} = \frac{1}{{1 + 55.56}} = 0.0177{/tex} It is given that, Vapour pressure of water, {tex}\left( {p_1^0} \right){/tex} = 12.3 kPa Applying the relation, {tex}\frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = {x_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{12.3 - {p_1}}}{{12.3}} = 0.0177 \Rightarrow 12.3 - {p_1} = 0.2177{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {p_1}{/tex} = 12.0823 = 12.08 kPa (approximately) Hence, the vapour pressure of the solution is 12.08 kPa.
solution_html:
1 molal solution means 1 mol of the solute is present in 1000 g of the solvent (water).
Molar mass of water = {tex}18{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Therefore, Number of moles present in 1000 g of water = {tex}\frac{{1000}}{{18}}{/tex}
= 55.56 mol
Therefore, mole fraction of the solute in the solution is
{tex}{x_2} = \frac{1}{{1 + 55.56}} = 0.0177{/tex}
It is given that,
Vapour pressure of water, {tex}\left( {p_1^0} \right){/tex} = 12.3 kPa
Applying the relation, {tex}\frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = {x_2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{12.3 - {p_1}}}{{12.3}} = 0.0177 \Rightarrow 12.3 - {p_1} = 0.2177{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {p_1}{/tex} = 12.0823
= 12.08 kPa (approximately)
Hence, the vapour pressure of the solution is 12.08 kPa.

question_text: Calculate the mass of a non-volatile solute (molar mass 40 g mol -1 ) which should be dissolved in 114 g octane to reduce its vapour pressure to 80%.
question_html: Calculate the mass of a non-volatile solute (molar mass 40 g mol-1) which should be dissolved in 114 g octane to reduce its vapour pressure to 80%.
solution_text: Let the vapour pressure of pure octane be {tex}p^0_1{/tex} Then, the vapour pressure of the octane after dissolving the non-volatile solute is {tex}\frac{80}{100}{/tex} {tex}p^0_1{/tex} = 0.8 Molar mass of solute, M 2 = 40 g mol -1 Mass of octane, w 1 = 114 g Molar mass of octane, {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18}\right), M_1{/tex} = {tex}8\times 12+18 \times 1{/tex} =114 {tex}\mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex} Applying the relation, {tex}\frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times M_1}{M_2 \times w_1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_1^0-\left(0.8 p_1\right)^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times 114}{40 \times 114}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.2 p_1^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.2 = {tex}\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex} w 2 = 8g Hence, the required mass of the solute is 8g.
solution_html:
Let the vapour pressure of pure octane be {tex}p^0_1{/tex}
Then, the vapour pressure of the octane after dissolving the non-volatile solute is {tex}\frac{80}{100}{/tex} {tex}p^0_1{/tex} = 0.8
Molar mass of solute, M2 = 40 g mol-1
Mass of octane, w1 = 114 g
Molar mass of octane, {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18}\right), M_1{/tex} = {tex}8\times 12+18 \times 1{/tex}
=114 {tex}\mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
Applying the relation,
{tex}\frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times M_1}{M_2 \times w_1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_1^0-\left(0.8 p_1\right)^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times 114}{40 \times 114}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.2 p_1^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.2 = {tex}\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex}
 w2 = 8g
Hence, the required mass of the solute is 8g.

question_text: A solution containing 30 g of non-volatile solute exactly in 90 g of water has a vapour pressure of 2.8 kPa at 298 K. Further, 18 g of water is then added to the solution and the new vapour pressure becomes 2.9 kPa at 298 K. Calculate: molar mass of the solute vapour pressure of water at 298 K.
question_html:
A solution containing 30 g of non-volatile solute exactly in 90 g of water has a vapour pressure of 2.8 kPa at 298 K. Further, 18 g of water is then added to the solution and the new vapour pressure becomes 2.9 kPa at 298 K. Calculate:

\frac{{2.9}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{30}}{{6M + 30}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.9}}{{p_1^0}} = 1 - \frac{{30}}{{6M + 30}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.9}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{6M + 30 - 30}}{{6M + 30}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.9}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{6M}}{{6M + 30}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{p_1^0}}{{2.9}} = \frac{{6M + 30}}{{6M}}................(ii){/tex} Dividing equation (i) by (ii), we have: {tex}\frac{{2.9}}{{2.8}} = \frac{{\frac{{5M + 30}}{{5M}}}}{{\frac{{6M + 30}}{{6M}}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.9}}{{2.8}} \times \frac{{6M + 30}}{6} = \frac{{5M + 30}}{5}{/tex} {tex}2.9 \times 5 \times \left( {6M + 30} \right) = 2.8 \times 6 \times \left( {5M + 30} \right){/tex} 87M + 435 = 84 M +504 3M = 69 M = 23 Therefore, the molar mass of the solute is {tex}23{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - subject: Chemistry
question_text: A 5% solution (by mass) of cane sugar in water has a freezing point of 271K. Calculate the freezing point of 5% glucose in water if the freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K.
question_html: A 5% solution (by mass) of cane sugar in water has a freezing point of 271K. Calculate the freezing point of 5% glucose in water if the freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K.
solution_text: Given,5% sugar solution means, w B = 5g w A = 95g,M B = 342g mol -1 Similarly, for 5% glucose solution, w B (glucose) = 5g w A = 95 g and M B (glucose) = 180 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta {T_f}{/tex} (canesugar) = 273.15K - 271K = 2.15K {tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B}{w_A}}}{/tex} {tex}2.15 = \frac{{{K_f} \times 5 \times 1000}}{{342 \times 95}}{/tex} {tex}{k_f} = \frac{{2.15 \times 342 \times 95}}{{5 \times 1000}} = 13.9707{/tex} similarly, for glucose {tex}\Delta {T_f}(glucose) = \frac{{13.9707 \times 5 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 95}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_f} = 4.085K{/tex} Freezing point of the solution = 273.15K - 4.085K = 269.065 K
solution_html:
Given,5% sugar solution means, wB = 5g
wA = 95g,MB = 342g mol-1
Similarly, for 5% glucose solution, wB(glucose) = 5g
wA = 95 g and MB(glucose) = 180 g mol-1
{tex}\Delta {T_f}{/tex}(canesugar) = 273.15K - 271K = 2.15K
{tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B}{w_A}}}{/tex}
{tex}2.15 = \frac{{{K_f} \times 5 \times 1000}}{{342 \times 95}}{/tex}
{tex}{k_f} = \frac{{2.15 \times 342 \times 95}}{{5 \times 1000}} = 13.9707{/tex}
similarly, for glucose
{tex}\Delta {T_f}(glucose) = \frac{{13.9707 \times 5 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 95}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {T_f} = 4.085K{/tex}
Freezing point of the solution
= 273.15K - 4.085K = 269.065 K

question_text: Two elements A and B form compounds having formula AB 2 and AB 4 . When dissolved in 20 g of benzene (C 6 H 6 ), 1 g of AB 2 lowers the freezing point by 2.3 K whereas 1.0 g of AB 4 lowers it by 1.3 K. The molar depression constant for benzene is 5.1 K kg mol -1 . Calculate atomic masses of A and B.
question_html: Two elements A and B form compounds having formula AB2 and AB4. When dissolved in 20 g of benzene (C6H6), 1 g of AB2 lowers the freezing point by 2.3 K whereas 1.0 g of AB4 lowers it by 1.3 K. The molar depression constant for benzene is 5.1 K kg mol-1. Calculate atomic masses of A and B.
solution_text: We know that {tex}{M_2} = \frac{{1000 \times {w_2} \times {k_f}}}{{\Delta {T_f} \times {w_1}}}{/tex} Then, {tex}{M_{A{B_2}}} = \frac{{1000 \times 1 \times 5.1}}{{2.3 \times 20}}{/tex} = {tex}110.87{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}{M_{A{B_4}}} = \frac{{1000 \times 1 \times 5.1}}{{1.3 \times 20}}{/tex} = {tex}196.15{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Now, we have the molar masses of {tex}A{B_2}{/tex} and {tex}A{B_4}{/tex} as {tex}110.87{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}} and 196.15{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} respectively. Let the atomic masses of A and B be x and y respectively. Now, we can write: {tex}x + 2y = 110.87................(i){/tex} {tex}x + 4y = 196.15................(ii){/tex} Subtracting equation (i) from (ii), we have 2y = 85.28 {tex}\Rightarrow \,y = 42.64{/tex} Putting the value of y=42.64 in equation (1), we have {tex}x + 2 \times 42.64 = 110.87{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \,x = 25.59{/tex} Hence, the atomic masses of A and B are 25.59 u and 42.64 u respectively.
solution_html:
We know that
{tex}{M_2} = \frac{{1000 \times {w_2} \times {k_f}}}{{\Delta {T_f} \times {w_1}}}{/tex}  
Then, {tex}{M_{A{B_2}}} = \frac{{1000 \times 1 \times 5.1}}{{2.3 \times 20}}{/tex}    
= {tex}110.87{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{M_{A{B_4}}} = \frac{{1000 \times 1 \times 5.1}}{{1.3 \times 20}}{/tex}
= {tex}196.15{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Now, we have the molar masses of {tex}A{B_2}{/tex} and {tex}A{B_4}{/tex} as {tex}110.87{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}} and 196.15{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} respectively.
Let the atomic masses of A and B be x and y respectively.
Now, we can write:
{tex}x + 2y = 110.87................(i){/tex}
{tex}x + 4y = 196.15................(ii){/tex}
Subtracting equation (i) from (ii), we have
2y = 85.28
{tex}\Rightarrow \,y = 42.64{/tex}
Putting the value of y=42.64 in equation (1), we have
{tex}x + 2 \times 42.64 = 110.87{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \,x = 25.59{/tex}
Hence, the atomic masses of A and B are 25.59 u and 42.64 u respectively.

question_text: At 300 K, 36 g of glucose present in a litre of its solution has an osmotic pressure of 4.98 bar. If the osmotic pressure of the solution is 1.52 bars at the same temperature, what would be its concentration?
question_html: At 300 K, 36 g of glucose present in a litre of its solution has an osmotic pressure of 4.98 bar. If the osmotic pressure of the solution is 1.52 bars at the same temperature, what would be its concentration?
solution_text: Here, T = 300 K {tex}\pi {/tex} = 1.52 bar R = 0.083 bar L {tex}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Applying the relation, {tex}\pi {/tex} = CRT {tex}C = \frac{\pi }{{RT}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.52\,bar}}{{0.083\,bar\,L\,{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 300\,k}}{/tex} = 0.061 mol Since the volume of the solution is 1 L, the concentration of the solution would be 0.061 M.
solution_html:
Here,
T = 300 K
{tex}\pi {/tex} = 1.52 bar
R = 0.083 bar L {tex}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Applying the relation,
{tex}\pi {/tex} = CRT
{tex}C = \frac{\pi }{{RT}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1.52\,bar}}{{0.083\,bar\,L\,{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 300\,k}}{/tex}

Since the volume of the solution is 1 L, the concentration of the solution would be 0.061 M.

question_text: Based on solute-solvent interactions, arrange the following in order of increasing solubility in n-octane and explain. Cyclohexane, KCl, CH 3 OH, CH 3 CN.
question_html: Based on solute-solvent interactions, arrange the following in order of increasing solubility in n-octane and explain. Cyclohexane, KCl, CH3OH, CH3CN.
solution_text: n-octane is a non-polar solvent. Therefore, the solubility of a non-polar solute is more than that of a polar solute in the n-octane. The order of increasing polarity is: Cyclohexane < {tex}C{H_3}CN < C{H_3}OH{/tex} < KCl Therefore, the order of increasing solubility is: KCl < {tex}C{H_3}OH{/tex} < {tex}C{H_3}CN{/tex} < Cyclohexane
