question_text: If the density of some lake water is 1.25 g mL -1 and contains 92 g of Na + ions per kg of water, calculate the molarity of Na + ions in the lake.
question_html: If the density of some lake water is 1.25 g mL-1 and contains 92 g of Na+ ions per kg of water, calculate the molarity of Na+ ions in the lake.
solution_text: Molar mass of {tex}Na = 23\;g\;{mol}^{-1}{/tex} No. of moles of {tex}{Na}^{+} = \frac{{92g}}{{23{\rm{ g mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}} = 4{\rm{ mole}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Density }=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{1000}{1.25}(\mathrm{~g}){/tex} {tex}V=800 \mathrm{ml}{/tex} {tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{\text { Number of moles }}{\text { Volume }(\mathrm{ml})} \times 1000{/tex} {tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{4 \times 1000}{800}=5 \text { moles }{/tex}
solution_html:
Molar mass of {tex}Na = 23\;g\;{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
No. of moles of {tex}{Na}^{+} = \frac{{92g}}{{23{\rm{ g mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}} = 4{\rm{ mole}}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Density }=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}{/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{1000}{1.25}(\mathrm{~g}){/tex}
{tex}V=800 \mathrm{ml}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{\text { Number of moles }}{\text { Volume }(\mathrm{ml})} \times 1000{/tex}
{tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{4 \times 1000}{800}=5 \text { moles }{/tex}

question_text: If the solubility product of CuS is 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 , calculate the maximum molarity of CuS in aqueous solution.
question_html: If the solubility product of CuS is 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16, calculate the maximum molarity of CuS in aqueous solution.
solution_text: Solubility product of CuS, {tex}{K_s}p = 6 \times {10^{ - 16}}{/tex} Let s be the solubility of CuS in {tex}mol{\rm{ }}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}CuS \leftrightarrow C{u^{2 + }} + {S^{2 + }}{/tex} Now, {tex} {K_s}p = \left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]\left[ {{S^{2 - }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l} = s \times s\\ = {s^2} \end{array}{/tex} Then, we have, {tex}{K_s}p = {s^2} = 6 \times {10^{ - 16}}{/tex} {tex}s = \sqrt {6 \times {{10}^{ - 16}}} = 2.45 \times {10^{ - 8}}mol{\rm{ }}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} Hence, the maximum molarity of CuS in an aqueous solution is {tex}2.45 \times {10^{ - 8}}mol{\rm{ }}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Solubility product of CuS, {tex}{K_s}p = 6 \times {10^{ - 16}}{/tex}
Let s be the solubility of CuS in {tex}mol{\rm{ }}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}CuS \leftrightarrow C{u^{2 + }} + {S^{2 + }}{/tex}
Now,{tex} {K_s}p = \left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]\left[ {{S^{2 - }}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{l} = s \times s\\ = {s^2} \end{array}{/tex}
Then, we have, {tex}{K_s}p = {s^2} = 6 \times {10^{ - 16}}{/tex}
{tex}s = \sqrt {6 \times {{10}^{ - 16}}} = 2.45 \times {10^{ - 8}}mol{\rm{ }}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Hence, the maximum molarity of CuS in an aqueous solution is {tex}2.45 \times {10^{ - 8}}mol{\rm{ }}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}

question_text: Nalorphene (C 19 H 21 NO 3 ), similar to morphine, is used to combat withdrawal symptoms in narcotic users. Dose of nalorphene generally given is 1.5 mg. Calculate the mass of 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m aqueous solution required for the above dose.
question_html: Nalorphene (C19H21NO3), similar to morphine, is used to combat withdrawal symptoms in narcotic users. Dose of nalorphene generally given is 1.5 mg. Calculate the mass of 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m aqueous solution required for the above dose.
solution_text: The molar mass of nalorphene {tex}{C_{19}}{H_{21}}N{O_3}{/tex} is given as: {tex}19 \times 12 + 21 \times 1 + 1 \times 14 + 3 \times 16 = 311\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} In {tex}1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} aqueous solution of nalorphene. 1 kg (1000 g) of water contains {tex}1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} mol = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 311\,g{/tex} = 0.4665 g Therefore, total mass of the solution = (1000 + 0.4665) g = 1000.4665 g This implies that the mass of the solution containing 0.4665 g of nalorphene is 1000.4665 g. Therefore, mass of the solution containing 1.5 mg of nalorphene is: {tex}\frac{{1000.4665 \times 1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.4665}}g{/tex} = 3.22 g Hence, the mass of aqueous solution required is 3.22g.
solution_html:
The molar mass of nalorphene {tex}{C_{19}}{H_{21}}N{O_3}{/tex} is given as:
{tex}19 \times 12 + 21 \times 1 + 1 \times 14 + 3 \times 16 = 311\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
In {tex}1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex}aqueous solution of nalorphene.
1 kg (1000 g) of water contains {tex}1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} mol = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 311\,g{/tex}
= 0.4665 g
Therefore, total mass of the solution = (1000 + 0.4665) g
= 1000.4665 g
This implies that the mass of the solution containing 0.4665 g of nalorphene is 1000.4665 g.
Therefore, mass of the solution containing 1.5 mg of nalorphene is:
{tex}\frac{{1000.4665 \times 1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.4665}}g{/tex}
= 3.22 g
Hence, the mass of aqueous solution required is 3.22g.

question_text: Calculate the amount of benzoic acid (C 6 H 5 COOH) required for preparing 250 mL of 0.15 M solution in methanol.
question_html: Calculate the amount of benzoic acid (C6H5COOH) required for preparing 250 mL of 0.15 M solution in methanol.
solution_text: 0.15 M solution means that 0.15 moles of C 6 H 5 COOH is present in 1 L = 1000 mL of the solution Molar mass of C 6 H 5 COOH = {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2\;=(7\times12)\;+(6\times1)+(2\times16)\;=122g/mol}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} 0.15 mole of benzoic acid {tex} = 0.15 \times 122g = 18.3g{/tex} Thus 1000 mL of solution contains benzoic acid = 18.3 g 250 mL of solution will contain benzoic acid {tex} = \frac{{18.3}}{{1000}} \times 250{/tex} = 4.575 g
solution_html:
0.15 M solution means that 0.15 moles of C6H5COOH is present in 1 L
= 1000 mL of the solution
Molar mass of C6H5COOH = {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2\;=(7\times12)\;+(6\times1)+(2\times16)\;=122g/mol}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} 0.15 mole of benzoic acid
{tex} = 0.15 \times 122g = 18.3g{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{18.3}}{{1000}} \times 250{/tex} = 4.575 g

question_text: The depression in freezing point of water observed for the same amount of acetic acid, trichloroacetic acid and trifluoroacetic acid increases in the order given above. Explain briefly.
question_html: The depression in freezing point of water observed for the same amount of acetic acid, trichloroacetic acid and trifluoroacetic acid increases in the order given above. Explain briefly.
solution_text: Among H, Cl, and F, H is least electronegative while F is most electronegative. Then, F can withdraw electrons towards itself due to -I effect more than Cl and H. Thus, trifluoroacetic acid can easily lose {tex}H^+{/tex} ions i.e., trifluoroacetic acid ionizes to the largest extent. Now, the more ions produced, the greater is the depression of the freezing point. Hence, the depression in the freezing point increases in the order: Acetic acid < trichloroacetic acid < trifluoroacetic acid.

solution_text: Molar mass of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2\;=(4\times12)\;+(7\times1)+(2\times16)\;+(1\;\times35.5)\;=122.5g/mol}{/tex} Number of moles of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{122.5}}{/tex} = 8.16 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -2 mole {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality of the solution (m) {tex}(m) = \frac{{8.16 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}mol}}{{250}} \times 1000\,g\,K{g^{ - 1}}{/tex} = 0.3264m If x is the degree of dissociation of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH then {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}}\limits_{n\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} }\limits_{n(x - 1)} CHClCOOH \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_{nx} CHCl\mathop {\mathop {CO{O^ - } + }\limits_{{H^ + }} }\limits_{nx}{/tex} To calculate Van't Hoff factor Total moles of particles = n[1 - x + x + x] = n [1 + x] {tex}i = \frac{{n(1 + x)}}{n} = 1 + x{/tex} We know that {tex}x = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_a}}}{x}}{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {\frac{{1.4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.3264}}}{/tex} = 0.065 i = 1 + 0.065 = 1.065 {tex}\Delta {T_f} = i{K_f}m{/tex} = 1.065 × 1.86 × 0.3264 = 0.65°

 {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2\;=(4\times12)\;+(7\times1)+(2\times16)\;+(1\;\times35.5)\;=122.5g/mol}{/tex}

question_text: 19.5 g of CH 2 FCOOH is dissolved in 500 g of water. The depression in the freezing point of water observed is 1.0°C. Calculate the Van't Hoff factor and dissociation constant of fluoroacetic acid.
question_html: 19.5 g of CH2FCOOH is dissolved in 500 g of water. The depression in the freezing point of water observed is 1.0°C. Calculate the Van't Hoff factor and dissociation constant of fluoroacetic acid.
solution_text: W 2 = 19.5 g W 1 = 500 g {tex} \Delta{/tex} T f (observed) = 1.0°C K f for water = 1.86 K Kg mol -1 {tex} {M_2}(observed) = \frac{{1000{K_f}{W_2}}}{{{W_1}\Delta {T_f}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1000 \times 1.86 \times 19.5}}{{500 \times 1.0}}{/tex} = 72.54 g mol -1 M 2 (calculated) = CH 2 FCOOH {tex}=\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2=(2\;\times12)\;+(3\times1)+(2\times16)+(1\times19)\;=78g/mol}{/tex} Van't Hoff factor {tex} (i) = \frac{{{M_2}(calculated)}}{{{M_2}(observed)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{78}}{{72.54}}{/tex} = 1.0753 Calculation of dissociation constant: Suppose degree of dissociation at the given concentration is x. Then {tex} \mathop {\mathop {C{H_2}FCOOH}\limits_{n\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} }\limits_{n(1 - x)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_2}F\,CO{O^ - }}\limits_0 }\limits_{nx} + \mathop {\mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_0 }\limits_{nx}{/tex} Total no. of moles = n(1 - x + x + x) = n (1 + x) {tex} i = \frac{{n(1 + x)}}{n} = 1 + x{/tex} x = i - 1 = 1.0753 - 1 = 0.0753 {tex} {K_a} = \frac{{\left[ {C{H_2}FCO{O^ - }} \right]\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}}{{\left[ {C{H_2}FCOOH} \right]}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{nx \times nx}}{{n(1 - x)}} = \frac{{n{x^2}}}{{1 - x}}{/tex} {tex} n = \frac{{19.5}}{{78}} \times \frac{1}{{500}} \times 1000{/tex} = 0.5 M {tex} {K_a} = \frac{{(0.5){{(0.0753)}^2}}}{{1 - 0.0753}} = 3.07 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex}
solution_html:
W2 = 19.5 g
W1 = 500 g
{tex} \Delta{/tex}Tf (observed) = 1.0°C
Kf for water = 1.86 K Kg mol-1
{tex} {M_2}(observed) = \frac{{1000{K_f}{W_2}}}{{{W_1}\Delta {T_f}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1000 \times 1.86 \times 19.5}}{{500 \times 1.0}}{/tex} = 72.54 g mol-1
M2(calculated) = CH2FCOOH
{tex}=\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2=(2\;\times12)\;+(3\times1)+(2\times16)+(1\times19)\;=78g/mol}{/tex}
Van't Hoff factor {tex} (i) = \frac{{{M_2}(calculated)}}{{{M_2}(observed)}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{78}}{{72.54}}{/tex} = 1.0753
Calculation of dissociation constant: Suppose degree of dissociation at the given concentration is x.
Then
{tex} \mathop {\mathop {C{H_2}FCOOH}\limits_{n\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} }\limits_{n(1 - x)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_2}F\,CO{O^ - }}\limits_0 }\limits_{nx} + \mathop {\mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_0 }\limits_{nx}{/tex}
Total no. of moles = n(1 - x + x + x)
= n (1 + x)
{tex} i = \frac{{n(1 + x)}}{n} = 1 + x{/tex}
x = i - 1
= 1.0753 - 1 = 0.0753
{tex} {K_a} = \frac{{\left[ {C{H_2}FCO{O^ - }} \right]\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}}{{\left[ {C{H_2}FCOOH} \right]}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{nx \times nx}}{{n(1 - x)}} = \frac{{n{x^2}}}{{1 - x}}{/tex}
{tex} n = \frac{{19.5}}{{78}} \times \frac{1}{{500}} \times 1000{/tex} = 0.5 M
{tex} {K_a} = \frac{{(0.5){{(0.0753)}^2}}}{{1 - 0.0753}} = 3.07 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex}

question_text: Vapour pressure of water at 293 K is 17.535 mm Hg. Calculate the vapour pressure of water at 293 K when 25 g of glucose is dissolved in 450 g of water.
question_html: Vapour pressure of water at 293 K is 17.535 mm Hg. Calculate the vapour pressure of water at 293 K when 25 g of glucose is dissolved in 450 g of water.
solution_text: Vapour pressure of water, {tex}p_1^0{/tex} = 17.535 mm of Hg Mass of glucose, w 2 = 25 g Mass of water, w 1 = 450 g We know that, Molar mass of glucose {tex}\left( {{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}} \right),{M_2} =( 6 \times 12) + (12 \times 1) + (6 \times 16){/tex} = 180 g mol -1 Molar mass of water, M 1 = 18 g mol -1 Then, number of moles of glucose, {tex}{n_2} = \frac{{25}}{{180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.139 mol And, number of moles of water {tex}{n_1} = \frac{{450}}{{18\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 25 mol We know that, {tex}\frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2} + {n_1}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{17.535 - {p_1}}}{{17.535}} = \frac{{0.139}}{{0.139 + 25}}{/tex} {tex}17.535 - {p_1} = \frac{{0.139 \times 17.535}}{{25.139}}{/tex} {tex}17.535{\rm{ }} - {p_1}{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}0.097{/tex} p 1 = 17.438 mm of Hg Hence, the vapour pressure of water is 17.44 mm of Hg.
solution_html:
Vapour pressure of water, {tex}p_1^0{/tex} = 17.535 mm of Hg
Mass of glucose, w2 = 25 g
Mass of water, w1 = 450 g
We know that,
Molar mass of glucose {tex}\left( {{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}} \right),{M_2} =( 6 \times 12) + (12 \times 1) + (6 \times 16){/tex}
= 180 g mol-1
Molar mass of water, M1 = 18 g mol-1
Then, number of moles of glucose, {tex}{n_2} = \frac{{25}}{{180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}
= 0.139 mol
And, number of moles of water {tex}{n_1} = \frac{{450}}{{18\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}
= 25 mol
We know that,
{tex}\frac{{p_1^0 - {p_1}}}{{p_1^0}} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2} + {n_1}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{17.535 - {p_1}}}{{17.535}} = \frac{{0.139}}{{0.139 + 25}}{/tex}
{tex}17.535 - {p_1} = \frac{{0.139 \times 17.535}}{{25.139}}{/tex}
{tex}17.535{\rm{ }} - {p_1}{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}0.097{/tex}
p1 = 17.438 mm of Hg
Hence, the vapour pressure of water is 17.44 mm of Hg.

question_text: Henry's law constant for the solubility of methane in benzene at 298 K is 4.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 mm Hg. Calculate the solubility of methane in benzene at 298 K under 760 mm Hg.
question_html: Henry's law constant for the solubility of methane in benzene at 298 K is 4.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 mm Hg. Calculate the solubility of methane in benzene at 298 K under 760 mm Hg.
solution_text: Here K H = 4.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 mm Hg P = 760 mm Hg Applying Henry's law P = K H x x = {tex}\frac{P}{{{K_H}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{760\,mm Hg}}{{4.27 \times {{10}^5}\,mm Hg}}{/tex} = 1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 i.e. mole fraction of methane in benzene = 1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 .
solution_html:
Here KH = 4.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 mm Hg
P = 760 mm Hg
Applying Henry's law
P = KHx
x = {tex}\frac{P}{{{K_H}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{760\,mm Hg}}{{4.27 \times {{10}^5}\,mm Hg}}{/tex} = 1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
i.e. mole fraction of methane in benzene = 1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3.

question_text: 100 g of liquid A (molar mass 140 g mol -1 ) was dissolved in 1000 g of liquid B (molar mass 180 g mol -1 ). The vapour pressure of pure liquid B was found to be 500 torr. Calculate the vapour pressure of pure liquid A and its vapour pressure in the solution if the total vapour pressure of the solution is 475 Torr.
question_html: 100 g of liquid A (molar mass 140 g mol-1) was dissolved in 1000 g of liquid B (molar mass 180 g mol-1). The vapour pressure of pure liquid B was found to be 500 torr. Calculate the vapour pressure of pure liquid A and its vapour pressure in the solution if the total vapour pressure of the solution is 475 Torr.
solution_text: Number of moles of liquid A, {tex}{n_A} = \frac{{100}}{{140}}mol{/tex} = 0.714 mol Number of moles of liquid B, {tex}{n_B} = \frac{{1000}}{{180}}mol{/tex} = 5.556 mol Then, mole fraction of A, {tex}{x_A} = \frac{{{n_A}}}{{{n_A} + {n_B}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.714}}{{0.714 + 5.556}}{/tex} = 0.114 And, mole fraction of B, {tex}{x_B} = 1 - 0.114{/tex} = 0.886 Vapour pressure of pure liquid B, {tex}p_1^0{/tex} = 500 torr Therefore, vapour pressure of liquid B in the solution, {tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B}{/tex} {tex}500 \times 0.886{/tex} = 443 torr Total vapour pressure of the solution, p total = 475 torr Therefore, Vapour pressure of liquid A in the solution, {tex}{p_A} = {p_{total}} - {p_B}{/tex} = 475 - 443 = 32 torr {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex} Now, {tex}p_A^0 = \frac{{{p_A}}}{{{x_A}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{32}}{{0.114}}{/tex} = 280.7 torr Hence, the vapour pressure of pure liquid A is 280.7 torr.
solution_html:
Number of moles of liquid A, {tex}{n_A} = \frac{{100}}{{140}}mol{/tex}
= 0.714 mol
Number of moles of liquid B, {tex}{n_B} = \frac{{1000}}{{180}}mol{/tex}
= 5.556 mol
Then, mole fraction of A, {tex}{x_A} = \frac{{{n_A}}}{{{n_A} + {n_B}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.714}}{{0.714 + 5.556}}{/tex}
= 0.114
And, mole fraction of B, {tex}{x_B} = 1 - 0.114{/tex}
= 0.886
Vapour pressure of pure liquid B, {tex}p_1^0{/tex} = 500 torr
Therefore, vapour pressure of liquid B in the solution,
{tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B}{/tex}
{tex}500 \times 0.886{/tex}
= 443 torr
Total vapour pressure of the solution, p total = 475 torr
Therefore, Vapour pressure of liquid A in the solution,
{tex}{p_A} = {p_{total}} - {p_B}{/tex}
= 475 - 443
= 32 torr
{tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex}
Now, {tex}p_A^0 = \frac{{{p_A}}}{{{x_A}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{32}}{{0.114}}{/tex}
= 280.7 torr
Hence, the vapour pressure of pure liquid A is 280.7 torr.

question_text: Benzene and toluene form ideal solution over the entire range of composition. The vapour pressure of pure benzene and naphthalene at 300 K are 50.71 mm Hg and 32.06 mm Hg respectively. Calculate the mole fraction of benzene in vapour phase if 80 g of benzene is mixed with 100 g of toluene.
question_html: Benzene and toluene form ideal solution over the entire range of composition. The vapour pressure of pure benzene and naphthalene at 300 K are 50.71 mm Hg and 32.06 mm Hg respectively. Calculate the mole fraction of benzene in vapour phase if 80 g of benzene is mixed with 100 g of toluene.
solution_text: Molar mass of benzene {tex}\left( {{C_6}{H_6}} \right) =( 6 \times 12 )+ (6 \times 1){/tex} = {tex}78\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Molar mass of toluene {tex}\left( {{C_6}{H_5}C{H_3}} \right) = (7 \times 12) +( 8 \times 1){/tex} = {tex}92\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Now, no. of moles present in 80 g of benzene = {tex}\frac{{80}}{{78}}mol\,{/tex} = 1.026 mol And, no. of moles present in 100 g of toluene = {tex}\frac{{100}}{{92}}mol\,{/tex} =1.087 mol Therefore, Mole fraction of benzene, {tex}{x_b} = \frac{{1.026}}{{1.025 + 1.087}}{/tex} = 0.486 And, mole fraction of toluene, {tex}{x_t} = 1 - 0.486{/tex} = 0.514 It is given that vapour pressure of pure benzene, {tex}p_b^0 = 50.71\,mm\,Hg{/tex} And, vapour pressure of pure toluene, {tex}p_1^0 = 32.06\,mm\,Hg{/tex} Therefore, partial vapour pressure of benzene, {tex}{p_b} = {x_b} \times {p_b}{/tex} {tex}= 0.486 \times 50.71{/tex} = 24.645 mm Hg And, partial vapour pressure of toluene, {tex}{p_1} = {x_t} \times {p_t}{/tex} {tex}= 0.514 \times 32.06{/tex} = 16.479 mm Hg Hence, mole fraction of benzene in vapour phase is given by: {tex}\frac{{{p_b}}}{{{p_b} + {p_t}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{24.645}}{{24.645 + 16.479}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{24.645}}{{41.124}}{/tex} = 0.599
solution_html:
Molar mass of benzene {tex}\left( {{C_6}{H_6}} \right) =( 6 \times 12 )+ (6 \times 1){/tex}
= {tex}78\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Molar mass of toluene {tex}\left( {{C_6}{H_5}C{H_3}} \right) = (7 \times 12) +( 8 \times 1){/tex}
= {tex}92\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Now, no. of moles present in 80 g of benzene = {tex}\frac{{80}}{{78}}mol\,{/tex}
= 1.026 mol
And, no. of moles present in 100 g of toluene = {tex}\frac{{100}}{{92}}mol\,{/tex}
=1.087 mol
Therefore, Mole fraction of benzene,  {tex}{x_b} = \frac{{1.026}}{{1.025 + 1.087}}{/tex}
= 0.486
And, mole fraction of toluene, {tex}{x_t} = 1 - 0.486{/tex} = 0.514
It is given that vapour pressure of pure benzene, {tex}p_b^0 = 50.71\,mm\,Hg{/tex}
And, vapour pressure of pure toluene, {tex}p_1^0 = 32.06\,mm\,Hg{/tex}
Therefore, partial vapour pressure of benzene, {tex}{p_b} = {x_b} \times {p_b}{/tex}
{tex}= 0.486 \times 50.71{/tex}
= 24.645 mm Hg
And, partial vapour pressure of toluene, {tex}{p_1} = {x_t} \times {p_t}{/tex}
{tex}= 0.514 \times 32.06{/tex}
= 16.479 mm Hg
Hence, mole fraction of benzene in vapour phase is given by:
{tex}\frac{{{p_b}}}{{{p_b} + {p_t}}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{24.645}}{{24.645 + 16.479}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{{24.645}}{{41.124}}{/tex}
= 0.599

question_text: The air is a mixture of a number of gases. The major components are oxygen and nitrogen with the approximate proportion of 20% is to 79% by volume at 298 K. The water is in equilibrium with air at a pressure of 10 atm. At 298 K if Henry's law constants for oxygen and nitrogen are 3.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 mm and 6.51 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 mm respectively, calculate the composition of these gases in water.
question_html: The air is a mixture of a number of gases. The major components are oxygen and nitrogen with the approximate proportion of 20% is to 79% by volume at 298 K. The water is in equilibrium with air at a pressure of 10 atm. At 298 K if Henry's law constants for oxygen and nitrogen are 3.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 mm and 6.51{tex}\times{/tex}107 mm respectively, calculate the composition of these gases in water.
solution_text: Percentage of oxygen (O 2 ) in air = 20 % Percentage of nitrogen (N 2 ) in air = 79% Also, it is given that water is in equilibrium with air at a total pressure of 10 atm, that is, (10×760)(10×760) mm Hg = 7600 mm Hg Therefore, Partial pressure of oxygen, {tex}{p_{{O_2}}} = \frac{{20}}{{100}} \times 7600\,mm\,Hg{/tex} = 1520 mm Hg Partial pressure of nitrogen, {tex}{p_{{N_2}}} = \frac{{79}}{{100}} \times 7600\,mm\,Hg{/tex} = 6004 mm Hg Now, according to Henry's law: {tex}p = {K_H}.x{/tex} For oxygen: {tex}{p_{{O_2}}} = {K_H}.{x_{{O_2}}}{/tex} {tex}{x_{{O_2}}} = \frac{{{p_{{O_2}}}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{1520\,mm\,Hg}}{{3.30 \times {{10}^7}\,mm\,Hg}} (Given: {K_H} = 3.30 \times {10^7}\,mm\,Hg){/tex} ) {tex}= 4.61 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} For nitrogen: {tex}{p_{{N_2}}} = {K_H}.{x_{{N_2}}}{/tex} {tex}{x_{{N_2}}} = \frac{{{p_{{N_2}}}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{6004\,mm\,Hg}}{{6.51 \times {{10}^7}\,mm\,Hg}}{/tex} ( Given : K H = 6.51 x 10 7 mm Hg) {tex}= 9.22 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} Hence, the mole fractions of oxygen and nitrogen in water are {tex}4.61 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} and {tex}9.22 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} respectively.
solution_html:
Percentage of oxygen (O2) in air = 20 %
Percentage of nitrogen (N2) in air = 79%
Also, it is given that water is in equilibrium with air at a total pressure of 10 atm, that is, (10×760)(10×760) mm Hg = 7600 mm Hg
Therefore,
Partial pressure of oxygen, {tex}{p_{{O_2}}} = \frac{{20}}{{100}} \times 7600\,mm\,Hg{/tex} = 1520 mm Hg
Partial pressure of nitrogen, {tex}{p_{{N_2}}} = \frac{{79}}{{100}} \times 7600\,mm\,Hg{/tex} = 6004 mm Hg
Now, according to Henry's law:
{tex}p = {K_H}.x{/tex}
For oxygen:
{tex}{p_{{O_2}}} = {K_H}.{x_{{O_2}}}{/tex}
{tex}{x_{{O_2}}} = \frac{{{p_{{O_2}}}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{1520\,mm\,Hg}}{{3.30 \times {{10}^7}\,mm\,Hg}} (Given: {K_H} = 3.30 \times {10^7}\,mm\,Hg){/tex})
{tex}= 4.61 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}
For nitrogen:
{tex}{p_{{N_2}}} = {K_H}.{x_{{N_2}}}{/tex}   
{tex}{x_{{N_2}}} = \frac{{{p_{{N_2}}}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{6004\,mm\,Hg}}{{6.51 \times {{10}^7}\,mm\,Hg}}{/tex}  ( Given : KH = 6.51 x 107 mm Hg)
{tex}= 9.22 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}
Hence, the mole fractions of oxygen and nitrogen in water are {tex}4.61 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} and {tex}9.22 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} respectively.

question_text: Determine the amount of CaCl 2 (i = 2.47) dissolved in 2.5 litre of water such that its osmotic pressure is 0.75 atm at 27 o C.
question_html: Determine the amount of CaCl2 (i = 2.47) dissolved in 2.5 litre of water such that its osmotic pressure is 0.75 atm at 27oC.
solution_text: We know that, {tex}\pi = i\frac{n}{V}RT{/tex} {tex}\pi = i\frac{w}{{MV}}RT{/tex} {tex}w = \frac{{\pi MV}}{{iRT}}{/tex} {tex}\pi = 0.75\,atm{/tex} V = 2.5 L i = 2.47 T = (27 + 273)K = 300 K Here, R = 0.0821 L atm {tex}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}M = (1 \times 40 )+ (2 \times 35.5){/tex} = 111 g {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}w = \frac{{0.75 \times 111 \times 2.5}}{{2.47 \times 0.0821 \times 300}}{/tex} = 3.42 g Hence, the required amount of CaCl 2 is 3.42 g.
solution_html:
We know that,
{tex}\pi = i\frac{n}{V}RT{/tex}
{tex}\pi = i\frac{w}{{MV}}RT{/tex}
{tex}w = \frac{{\pi MV}}{{iRT}}{/tex}
{tex}\pi = 0.75\,atm{/tex}
V = 2.5 L
i = 2.47
T = (27 + 273)K = 300 K
Here,
R = 0.0821 L atm {tex}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}M = (1 \times 40 )+ (2 \times 35.5){/tex}
= 111 g {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}w = \frac{{0.75 \times 111 \times 2.5}}{{2.47 \times 0.0821 \times 300}}{/tex}
= 3.42 g
Hence, the required amount of CaCl2 is 3.42 g.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Determine the osmotic pressure of a solution prepared by dissolving 25 mg of K 2 SO 4 in 2 litre of water at 25°C, assuming that it is completely dissociated.
question_html: Determine the osmotic pressure of a solution prepared by dissolving 25 mg of K2SO4 in 2 litre of water at 25°C, assuming that it is completely dissociated.
solution_text: K 2 SO 4 dissolved = 25 mg = 0.025 g Volume of solution = 2 litre T = 25°C = 25 + 273 = 298 K Molar mass of K 2 SO 4 = {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2=(1\;\times39)\;+(1\times32)+(1\times16)\;=174g/mol}{/tex} As K 2 SO 4 dissociates completely as {tex}{K_2}S{O_4} \to 2{K^ + } + SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} i.e. ions produced (i) = 3 {tex}\pi = i\,CRT{/tex} {tex}\pi = i \times \frac{W}{M} \times \frac{1}{V}RT{/tex} {tex}= 3 \times \frac{{0.025g}}{{174\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}} \times \frac{1}{{2L}} \times 0.0821L{/tex} atm K -1 mol -1 × 298 K = 5.27 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -3 atm
solution_html:
K2SO4 dissolved = 25 mg = 0.025 g
Volume of solution = 2 litre
T = 25°C = 25 + 273 = 298 K
Molar mass of K2SO4 ={tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2=(1\;\times39)\;+(1\times32)+(1\times16)\;=174g/mol}{/tex}
As K2SO4 dissociates completely as {tex}{K_2}S{O_4} \to 2{K^ + } + SO_4^{2 - }{/tex}
i.e. ions produced (i) = 3
{tex}\pi = i\,CRT{/tex}
{tex}\pi = i \times \frac{W}{M} \times \frac{1}{V}RT{/tex}
{tex}= 3 \times \frac{{0.025g}}{{174\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}} \times \frac{1}{{2L}} \times 0.0821L{/tex} atm K-1 mol-1 × 298 K
= 5.27 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-3 atm
